一小粒装有近地小行星Ryugu原始碎片的小胶囊于今天下午（12月5日）降落在偏远且崎omer的伍默拉禁酒区，距离南澳大利亚州首府阿德莱德西北约310英里（500公里）。
  日本的Hayabusa2任务从2018年6月至2019年11月近距离研究了3,000英尺（900米）的龙谷，将样本从地球抓获了数百万英里。
   Hayabusa2的前身是第一个将太空岩石样品运回家的人，2010年交付了几颗石质小行星Itokawa。但原始的Hayabusa（日语为“百富勤猎鹰”）返回了不到1毫克的材料。 Hayabusa2的赏金预计会超过100毫克（0.0035盎司），其样品来自一种非常不同的小行星-一种原始的C型＆＃34;富含水和含碳有机化合物的太空岩石。
  ＆＃34;构成地球，海洋和生命的物质存在于形成我们太阳系的原始云中。在早期的太阳系中，这些材料接触并能够在同一父对象内发生化学相互作用。日本航空航天局（JAXA）官员在Hayabusa2的概述中写道。
 ＆＃34;直到今天，这些相互作用仍保留在原始物体（C型小行星）中，因此从这些物体返回的样本进行分析将阐明太阳系的起源和演化以及生命的构成要素，＆＃34;他们补充说。
 在地球上收集样本是关键。全世界装备精良的实验室中的科学家可以比Hayabusa2或任何其他深空探测器本身更详细地检查宇宙岩石。退回材料的纯度也是主要卖点。研究人员已经可以使用许多陨石，但是这些＆quot;免费样品＆quot;＆quot;小行星通过地球大气层旅行以及它们在地球表面的停留时间已大大改变。
    1,340磅（690千克）Hayabusa2号航天器于2014年12月发射升空，并于2018年6月27日与崎R的Ryugu会合，开始了史诗般的探索运动。 
Hayabusa2详细观察了Ryugu，并在小行星表面上部署了多个微型探针-几个微小的跳跃式漫游车和一个称为MASCOT（移动小行星表面侦察员）的微波大小着陆器，该着陆器由德国航空航天中心与法国航天局CNES。
 Hayabusa2的主要航天器自己两次走到了Ryugu的表面，两次都捕获了样本。在这些操作的第一阶段中，2019年2月，Hayabusa2挖出了一些表面材料。那年四月，飞船发射了5.5磅重的炮弹。 （2.5公斤）铜射弹在龙古（Ryugu），向小行星表面爆炸了33英尺宽（10 m）的环形山。然后，那年7月，探员俯冲下来并收集了一些最近挖出的泥土和岩石。
 Hayabusa2将这两个样本分开放置，因此科学家将能够比较两种截然不同的环境中的物质-龙宫的表面受太空辐射风化，小行星的保护深度更深。
   在获得这些样本后，Hayabusa2于2019年11月离开了Ryugu并返回了家。今年11月26日，Hayabusa2距离地球约220万英里（360万公里），该探测器以关键的轨迹细化燃烧射击了其发动机。 JAXA官员在燃烧后的更新中写道，该动作使Hayabusa2驶向Woomera上方6英里宽（10 km）的天空部分-精确地相当于从0.6英里（1 km）以外的地方瞄准一只瓢虫。
 JAXA官员说，Hayabusa2在周五晚（12月4日）发布了16英寸宽（40厘米）的返回舱，距离我们的星球约137,000英里（220,000公里）。然后，主要航天器进行了另一次发动机燃烧，以使其远离地球，因为它的工作尚未完成：JAXA最近批准了Hayabusa2的扩展任务，该任务将在2026年由小型小行星（98943）2001 CC21飞行，并与另一个空间会合摇滚乐，1998年KY26，2031年。
 但是，返回舱没有自身的推进系统，却一直向着地球发射。它于下午12:28到达大气层。美国东部标准时间（格林尼治标准时间1728），预计时速约为26,840 mph（43,190 kph）。几分钟后，这艘小型飞船展开了降落伞，当时它离地面约6英里（10公里），并于下午12:47降落。美国东部时间（1747 GMT； JST凌晨2:47和澳大利亚当地时间12月6日凌晨4:17），JAXA官员说。
 救援人员乘坐直升飞机前往搜寻胶囊，最终于下午2:47发现。美国东部标准时间（1947 GMT）。 
今天（12/6）JST下午04:47，由于搜寻直升机，我们在计划的着陆区发现了一个胶囊舱！ 2020年12月5日
 经过安全检查后，Hayabusa2小组成员将把它运到日本JAXA的外星样品处理中心。该设施于2008年完工，是专门为容纳和研究太空任务带回家的宇宙材料而设计的。
 然后，一些Ryugu的材料将进入世界各地的实验室，在那里科学家们将对其进行研究，以获取有关太阳系初期和地球生命的增长的线索。
    Hayabusa2的带领下，很快将有其他几艘航天器，将自己的天堂碎片降落到地球上。
 例如，中国的Chang娥五号月球着陆器本周从月球表面挖出了原始物质。该样本定于12月16日或12月17日降落在内蒙古。今年10月，NASA的OSIRIS-REx航天器捕获了另一颗富含碳的近地小行星1,640个大样本。 -宽（500 m）Bennu。如果一切按计划进行，OSIRIS-REx的返回舱将于2023年9月降落在犹他州。
 Hayabusa2和OSIRIS-REx的目标大致相同，并且两个任务团队在过去几年中一直在广泛合作以帮助实现它们。两个团队的成员都说，这种合作将扩展到触地得分后样品的共享。
 此外，今年7月，美国宇航局（NASA）推出了2020年火星漫游者恒心计划，这是一项复杂，长期的将火星样本运送到地球的长期运动的第一步，该计划将于2021年2月降落在红色星球上。搜寻生命的漫游车的许多任务之一是收集和缓存数十个样本，美国宇航局和欧洲航天局将携手合作，有望最早于2031年将其带回家。 
JAXA正在开展自己的火星样本返回项目-一个名为火星卫星探索（MMX）的任务，该任务计划于2024年发射。MMX将获取火卫一的样本，火卫一是红色星球的两个小卫星之一， 并将它们带到地球进行分析。  编者注：这个故事在下午3:10更新。 美国东部时间12月5日报道，Hayabusa2小组发现了返回舱。  迈克·沃尔（Mike Wall）是＆＃34;的作者 在那里＆＃34; （大中央出版社，2018年;卡尔·泰特（Karl Tate）插图），一本关于寻找外星生命的书。 在Twitter @michaeldwall上关注他。 在Twitter @Spacedotcom或Facebook上关注我们。  加入我们的太空论坛，继续谈论最新任务，夜空等等的太空！ 如果您有新闻提示，更正或评论，请发送电子邮件至：community@space.com。
note_en:
A small capsule bearing pristine pieces of the   near-Earth asteroid Ryugu touched down early this afternoon (Dec. 5) within the remote and rugged Woomera Prohibited Area, about 310 miles (500 kilometers) northwest of the South Australian capital of Adelaide.
  The samples were snagged millions of miles from Earth by Japan's   Hayabusa2 mission, which studied the 3,000-foot-wide (900 meters) Ryugu up close from June 2018 to November 2019.
   Hayabusa2's predecessor was the first to haul space-rock samples home, delivering pieces of the stony asteroid Itokawa in 2010. But   the original Hayabusa (Japanese for "peregrine falcon") returned less than 1 milligram of material. Hayabusa2's bounty is expected to exceed 100 mg (0.0035 ounces), and its samples come from a very different kind of asteroid — a primitive "C-type" space rock rich in water and carbon-containing organic compounds.
  "The materials that formed the Earth, its oceans and life were present in the primordial cloud from which our solar system formed. In the early solar system, these materials were in contact and able to chemically interact within the same parent objects," Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) officials wrote in an   overview of Hayabusa2.
 "These interactions are retained even today in primitive bodies (C-type asteroids), so returning samples from these bodies for analysis will elucidate the origins and evolution of the solar system and the building blocks of life," they added.
 Having the samples here on Earth is key; scientists in well-equipped labs around the world can scrutinize the cosmic rock in far greater detail than Hayabusa2, or any other probe on its own in deep space, ever could. The returned material's purity is also a major selling point. Researchers already have access to many meteorites, but these "free samples" of   asteroids have been significantly altered by their trip through Earth's atmosphere and their time on our planet's surface.
    The 1,340-lb. (690 kilograms) Hayabusa2 spacecraft launched in December 2014 and rendezvoused with the rugged Ryugu on June 27, 2018, kicking off an epic exploration campaign.
 Hayabusa2 observed Ryugu in detail and also deployed multiple miniprobes onto the asteroid's surface — several   tiny, hopping rovers and a microwave-sized lander called MASCOT (Mobile Asteroid Surface Scout), which was provided by the German Aerospace Center in collaboration with the French space agency CNES.
 The main Hayabusa2 spacecraft made two trips of its own to Ryugu's surface, both times to snag samples. During the first of these operations, in February 2019, Hayabusa2 scooped up some surface material. In April of that year, the spacecraft fired a 5.5-lb. (2.5 kg) copper projectile at Ryugu,   blasting a 33-foot-wide (10 m) crater into the asteroid's surface. Then, that July, the probe swooped down and collected some of this recently excavated dirt and rock.
 Hayabusa2 kept these two samples separate, so scientists will be able to compare material from two very different environments — Ryugu's surface, which is weathered by space radiation, and the asteroid's more protected depths.
   With these samples secured, Hayabusa2 left Ryugu in November 2019 and headed home. On Nov. 26 of this year, when Hayabusa2 was about 2.2 million miles (3.6 million kilometers) from Earth, the probe fired its engines in a key trajectory-refining burn. The maneuver put Hayabusa2 on course toward a 6-mile-wide (10 km) slice of sky over Woomera — the precision equivalent of targeting a ladybug from 0.6 miles (1 km) away, JAXA officials   wrote in a post-burn update.
 Hayabusa2 released the 16-inch-wide (40 centimeters) return capsule on Friday night (Dec. 4), at a distance of about 137,000 miles (220,000 km) from our planet, JAXA officials said. The main spacecraft then conducted another engine burn to head away from Earth, for its work is not done: JAXA recently approved an   extended mission for Hayabusa2, which will fly by the small asteroid (98943) 2001 CC21 in 2026 and rendezvous with yet another space rock, 1998 KY26, in 2031.
 But the return capsule, which has no propulsion system of its own, kept barreling toward Earth. It hit the atmosphere at 12:28 p.m. EST (1728 GMT) today,  at an expected speed of roughly 26,840 mph (43,190 kph). The little craft deployed its parachute a few minutes later, when it was about 6 miles (10 km) above the ground, and touched down around 12:47 p.m. EST (1747 GMT; 2:47 a.m. JST and 4:17 a.m. local Australian time on Dec. 6), JAXA officials said.
 Recovery crews headed out in a helicopter to search for the capsule, ultimately  spotting it at 2:47 p.m. EST (1947 GMT).
 Today (12/6) at 04:47 JST, as a result of the helicopter search, we found a capsule in the planned landing area! December 5, 2020
 After securing and inspecting the craft, Hayabusa2 team members will transport it to JAXA's Extraterrestrial Sample Curation Center in Japan. This facility, which was completed in 2008, was designed specifically to house and study cosmic material brought home by space missions.
 Some of the Ryugu material will then make its way to labs around the world, where scientists will study it for clues about the solar system's early days and the rise of life on Earth.
    Several other spacecraft will soon follow Hayabusa2's lead, bringing their own pieces of heaven down to Earth.
 China's   Chang'e 5 lunar lander, for example, scooped up pristine material from the moon's surface this week. This sample is scheduled to land in Inner Mongolia on Dec. 16 or Dec. 17. And in October of this year, NASA's OSIRIS-REx spacecraft   snagged a big sample of another carbon-rich near-Earth asteroid, the 1,640-foot-wide (500 m) Bennu. OSIRIS-REx's return capsule will land in Utah in September 2023, if all goes according to plan.
 The goals of Hayabusa2 and OSIRIS-REx are broadly similar, and the two mission teams have been   working together extensively over the past few years to help achieve them. That collaboration will extend to the sharing of samples after touchdown, members of both teams have said.
 In addition, the first stage of a complex, long-term campaign to haul Mars samples to Earth kicked off this past July with the launch of NASA's   Mars 2020 rover Perseverance, which will land on the Red Planet in February 2021. Among the life-hunting rover's many tasks is the collection and caching of several dozen samples, which NASA and the European Space Agency will work together to bring home, potentially as early as 2031.
 JAXA is working on its own Mars sample-return project — a mission called Martian Moons Exploration (MMX), which is   scheduled to launch in 2024. MMX will grab samples of Phobos, one of the Red Planet's two small moons, and bring them to Earth for analysis.
 Editor's note: This story was updated at 3:10 p.m. EST on Dec. 5 to report that the Hayabusa2 team had found the return capsule.
 Mike Wall is the author of "   Out There " (Grand Central Publishing, 2018; illustrated by Karl Tate), a book about the search for alien life. Follow him on Twitter @michaeldwall. Follow us on Twitter @Spacedotcom or Facebook.
 Join our Space Forums to keep talking space on the latest missions, night sky and more! And if you have a news tip, correction or comment, let us know at:  community@space.com.

note:
Slack作为硅谷劣等宠儿的生活于2016年11月2日结束。那时，这家暴发户通信初创公司在《纽约时报》上向微软发布了一封公开信，为这家科技巨头提供了对工作场所聊天软件世界的真诚的“欢迎” 。场合是Microsoft推出了Teams，这是Slack的克隆版本，将与公司流行的Office 365产品套件捆绑在一起。
 Slack在信中警告微软，“ Slack将继续存在”，并补充说：“我们才刚刚起步。”但是四年后，当时的400万用户将增加到仅1200万，而微软（在不增加价格的情况下将Teams添加到365捆绑包中）将Microsoft从零增加到1.15亿用户。
 这种差异有助于解释Slack为什么在本周将自己出售给Salesforce。这笔交易使Slack的价值达到277亿美元，过去一年的收入为8.33亿美元，这在很大程度上引起了欢呼。 （本·汤普森在这里为Salesforce和Slack都提供了机会的绝好选择。）
  但这也感觉就像时代的终结，一个时代，工人获得了将自己的工具带到办公室并自行决定如何完成工作的新能力。 Slack首先是希望加速工作的小型团队取得成功，并且经常被早期采用而拖入组织。但是如今，整合浪潮使人们的实际选择减少了。
 智能手机在2010年代初期的兴起带来了工作场所生产力工具的新增长，这使之前的一切变得扑朔迷离。 Box和Dropbox带来了轻松的文件存储和共享。 Evernote引入了无处不在的，云同步笔记的想法。 Sunrise创建了一个更具社交性的日历，而Mailbox和Acompli重新设计了手机电子邮件。
 Slack在本世纪中叶开始涉足这一话题，几乎立即成为有史以来增长最快的企业软件工具。 2015年，就在发布18个月后，Slack报道每天有超过100万用户，而这个数字在企业软件中是闻所未闻的。
 它具有丰富的背景知识-失败的视频游戏Glitch的最后一击-在Stewart Butterfield中，科技界最迷人的创始人之一。它还有一个大胆的建议：它将“杀死电子邮件”-或至少减少对它的依赖-它将通过将数百种其他服务集成到实时工作聊天中来实现，组织的全知命令控制台。 
该公司体现出一种信念，即在硅谷如此普遍，即最终会赢得最好的产品。该公司在致微软的公开信中写道：“开发一种可以显着改善人们沟通方式的产品，需要一定的思想和技巧，这在企业软件开发中并不常见。” “与您要复制的单个软件功能相比，帮助公司真正转型以利用这种工作转变的优势甚至更加重要。”
  但是，如果过去四年有一个教训，那就是通过贴上Slack的基本设计，周到和精湛的技术只能使该公司达到微软的10％左右。在其公开信中，Slack著名地告诉微软：“你必须以爱来做到这一点。”看看Slack的规模，到2020年，这个想法似乎很可笑。爱与它有什么关系？
 问题是，我讨厌这是Slack的结果。我喜欢出色的生产力工具，并且扎根Slack到某天变得与公司大肆宣传一样出色。 （也许它仍然会：与大多数巨头一样，Salesforce在成功收购方面也有喜忧参半的记录，但似乎有些欣欣向荣。当我在Twitter上问到这个问题时，人们有很多美好的事情要说说收购后的Heroku。）
 但是，Slack作为一家独立公司而取得成功的努力令人遗憾地反映了许多企业生产力方面的一次性创新者。 Mailbox死了，Acompli卖给了Microsoft，成为了Outlook的移动应用程序。 Evernote是它以前的自我的淡淡阴影。在那个早期的队列中，只有Box和Dropbox成为（并且仍然保持）上市公司。
 为什么会这样呢？为了获得一些见识，我打电话给Box的和CEO可亲的首席执行官Aaron Levie。在列维（Levie）的讲述中-他还写了一篇有关Slack收购的博文-全部归结为销售。他告诉我说，工人总有一天会选择所有自己的工具的想法总是一个幻想，部分原因是大多数工人甚至都不愿考虑他们的工具。在这样的世界中，获胜的应用几乎总是一个拥有庞大而庞大的销售队伍的应用。
  Levie告诉我：“企业的现实是，您可以拥有最好的产品，但这还不够好。” “您需要分配。而Salesforce所拥有的-他们有采购官员，他们有财务人员。他们拥有与您互动所需的所有设备以销售软件，并且为全球排名前十万的公司提供了它们。”
 Levie看好这笔收购，因为这使Slack和Salesforce处于更加均衡的地位。 
他说：“微软拥有的唯一优势就是分销，因此现在他们已经抵消了微软拥有的优势。” “突然之间，他们实际上可以实现机会的最终承诺，因为他们的销售人员数量可以超过10倍，可以将其分发给世界各地的公司。”
  假设Levie是对的-我不会打赌他-这意味着中期工作的未来将越来越多地在三大巨头之间进行选择：微软，Salesforce和Google（排在第三）。这样一来，工人选择生产力工具的黄金时代似乎即将结束。
 这并不是说现任公司不会总是面对新的挑战者。但是我不知道Slack原来的低上限是否会对当前其他一些快速增长的生产力公司产生影响。 Slack的销售是否应该减少我们对Airtable，Notion或Coda的期望？别误会我的意思-我相信他们的投资者会先拿回他们的钱，然后再收回一些钱-但是他们是否有真正的前途未卜？
 如果没有，那么生产力市场将与互联网上从应用程序商店到搜索引擎再到社交网络的任何其他空间一样巩固。而且随着我们的政府反托拉斯监管机构在经过长时间的休眠之后开始苏醒，我想知道他们是否有话要说。
note_en:
Slack’s life as an underdog darling of Silicon Valley ended on November 2nd, 2016. That’s when the upstart communication startup  published an open letter to Microsoft in  The  New York Times, offering the tech giant an insincere “welcome” to the world of workplace chat software. The occasion was  Microsoft’s launch of Teams, a Slack clone that would come bundled with the company’s popular Office 365 suite of products.
 In its letter, Slack warned Microsoft that “Slack is here to stay,” adding, “We’re just getting started.” But the 4 million users it had at the time would increase to just 12 million four years later, while Microsoft — which added Teams to its 365 bundle without increasing the price —  took Teams from zero to 115 million users.
 That disparity helps to explain why  Slack sold itself this week to Salesforce. The deal, which values Slack at $27.7 billion on revenues of $833 million over the past year, has largely been greeted with cheers. (Ben Thompson  offers a typically excellent rundown of the opportunity here for both Salesforce and Slack.)
  But it also feels like the end of an era, one where workers gained new power to bring their own tools to the office and decide for themselves how they wanted to get work done. Slack first succeeded with small teams who wanted to accelerate their work and was often dragged into organizations by early adoption. But today, waves of consolidation are leaving people with fewer real choices.
 The rise of smartphones in the early 2010s brought with it a new surge of workplace productivity tools that made mincemeat of everything that had come before them. Box and Dropbox brought easy file storage and sharing. Evernote introduced the idea of ubiquitous, cloud-synchronized note-taking. Sunrise created a more social calendar, while Mailbox and Acompli reimagined email for the mobile phone.
 Slack tiptoed into the conversation in the middle of the decade and almost immediately became the fastest-growing enterprise software tool of all time. In 2015, just 18 months after it launched,  Slack reported having more than 1 million daily users — a figure then unheard of in enterprise software.
 It had a great backstory — a last-ditch pivot from a failed video game called  Glitch — and, in Stewart Butterfield, one of the tech world’s most charming founders. It also had a bold pitch: it was going to “kill email” — or, at the very least, reduce your reliance on it — and it would do so by integrating hundreds of other services into real-time work chat, creating a kind of all-knowing command console for your organization.
 The company embodied the belief, so common in Silicon Valley, that the best product would win in the end. “Building a product that allows for significant improvements in how people communicate requires a degree of thoughtfulness and craftsmanship that is not common in the development of enterprise software,” the company wrote in its open letter to Microsoft. “How far you go in helping companies truly transform to take advantage of this shift in working is even more important than the individual software features you are duplicating.”
  And yet, if there’s a lesson of the past four years, it’s that thoughtfulness and craftsmanship only got the company about 10 percent as far as Microsoft did by copy-pasting Slack’s basic design. In its open letter, Slack famously told Microsoft: “You’ve got to do this with love.” In 2020, looking at Slack’s size, the idea seems laughable. What’s love got to do with it?
 The thing is, I  hate that this was the outcome for Slack. I love good productivity tools and was rooting for Slack to someday become as good as the company hyped it up to be. (And perhaps it still will: like most giants, Salesforce has a mixed track record when it comes to the success of its acquisitions, but some seem to be thriving. When I asked about this on Twitter, people had  a lot of good things to say about post-acquisition Heroku.)
 But Slack’s struggle to succeed as an independent company sadly mirrors that of many one-time innovators in enterprise productivity. Mailbox died, and Acompli sold to Microsoft where it became the mobile Outlook app. Evernote is a pale shadow of its former self. Of that early cohort, only Box and Dropbox became — and still remain — public companies.
 Why is this the case? To get some insight, I called up Aaron Levie, Box’s affable CEO. In Levie’s telling — and he also wrote  a blog post about the Slack acquisition — it all comes down to sales. The idea that workers would someday choose all their own tools was always a fantasy, he told me, in part because most workers don’t even want to think about their tools. In such a world, the winning app will almost always be one with a giant, er... salesforce behind it.
  “The reality with the enterprise is that you can have the best product, but that’s not good enough,” Levie told me. “You need distribution. And what Salesforce has — they have the procurement officers, they have the finance people. They have all of the apparatus you need to interact with to sell software, and they have it for the top 100,000 corporations around the world.”
 Levie is bullish on the acquisition because it puts Slack and Salesforce on more even ground.
 “The only advantage Microsoft has is distribution, and so now they’ve neutralized the advantage that Microsoft has had,” he said. “All of a sudden, they can actually fulfill the ultimate promise of the opportunity, because they have 10 times the amount of salespeople that can go distribute this thing into corporations around the world.”
  Assuming Levie is right — and I wouldn’t bet against him — that means the medium-term future of work is increasingly a choice between three giants: Microsoft, Salesforce, and (in a distant third) Google. And with that, the golden age of worker choice in productivity tools seems to be coming to an end.
 That’s not to say that the incumbents won’t always face new challengers. But I wonder whether the low ceiling that Slack turned out to have has implications for some of the other fast-growing productivity companies of the current moment. Should Slack’s sale diminish our expectations for Airtable or Notion or Coda? Don’t get me wrong — I’m confident their investors will all get their money back, and then some — but do they have a real future outside the arms of a monolith?
 If not, then the productivity market will become as consolidated as any number of other spaces on the internet, from app stores to search engines to social networks. And as our government antitrust regulators begin to awaken after a long period of hibernation, I wonder if they’ll have anything to say about it.
title: 微软如何粉碎Slack

疫苗可能指日可待，但是要等多久才能打针？卫生官员正在考虑疫苗时间表，以使某些美国人优先于其他人。如果您是健康的美国人，则可能需要等待几个月才能轮到您。
 为了正确理解这一点，我们与Surgo Foundation和Ariadne Labs合作，使用他们的疫苗工具来计算每个州和县（以及您可能适合的人群）需要疫苗的人数。
   你几岁？您住在哪个县？您从事这些职业吗？您有与Covid相关的健康风险吗？
 根据您的风险状况，我们认为您在全美的人群中排在后面。
 说到，我们认为您落后于您所在州风险较高的其他人。
  如果排队的人数约为100人，那么您将处于以下位置：
    上述命令是一种可能性，将疾病控制和预防中心免疫实践咨询委员会的建议与美国国家科学，工程和医学研究院的更全面建议结合在一起。 
最终顺序尚未确定，取决于成功疫苗是否已针对每个组进行适当测试。如果未为18岁以下的人群批准早期疫苗，则可能会跳过儿童。各州也可以设定优先级，但是很可能会遵循最终的C.D.C.建议。
 我们将如何快速完成最后一行也是一个悬而未决的问题。
 尽管本月该国数百万医护人员可以接种疫苗，但最杰出的疫苗候选者需要一条超冷的分销链，该链还无法覆盖每个美国人。各国还需要购买更多的个人防护设备，并在大流行病中建立与社会保持距离的大规模疫苗接种点，这可能会减慢一切。
 Surgo基金会的联合创始人兼执行董事Sema Sgaier博士说：“令人难以置信的是，我们在如此短的时间内就能获得高水平的初始效力疫苗。”
 她补充说：“但疫苗暂时不会成为万灵药。”
  部署的依据是谁的风险最高，以及我们如何最有效地降低传播并减少死亡。 NASEM建议，年龄在18到30岁之间的成年人应该早点来，因为他们可能会导致更多的无症状传播。
 头几个月后可能会提供足够的疫苗，以完全跳过阶段并开始为所有人接种疫苗。但是，批准，制造或分销方面的任何延误都可能意味着明年大部分时间里，某些美国人会优先考虑其他人。 
范德比尔特大学医学院传染病学教授威廉·沙夫纳博士（William Schaffner博士）说：“我认为公共卫生领域没有人认为我们有能力在短短几个月内处理每个人的能力和后勤能力。” 。
 在像美国这样分裂的国家，这一进程似乎不可避免地会政治化。我们中的某些人应该优先于其他人的想法很可能与美国的第一手自由主义精神背道而驰，这是对戴口罩的法律的蔑视，并拒绝了支持阴谋论和谣言的科学建议。
 包括美国医学协会在内的几个团体争辩说，有230万被监禁的人应该在其他健康的美国人之前接种疫苗。这是有充分理由的：根据对州和联邦监狱的研究，监狱中的人患Covid-19的风险要高5.5倍。在监狱里进行社交疏远通常也是不可能的。
 监狱中的暴发也很容易传播到社区。一项研究发现，到4月中旬，伊利诺伊州大约有六分之一的冠状病毒病例与库克县监狱释放的人有联系。
 关于囚犯可以在普通美国人之前接种疫苗的想法已经面临挫折。科罗拉多州州长贾里德·波利斯（Jared Polis）本周说：“在没有犯任何罪行的人面前，这是不可能的。”
 Sgaier博士说：“无论人们选择哪种标准，总会有人对此感到不满意。” “我们绝对应该期望推后，人们对此过程不满意。那将是政治上的。”
 在被监禁和高风险美国人占较大比例的州，包括许多南部州，如阿拉巴马州，路易斯安那州，德克萨斯州和密西西比州，优先级似乎尤其明显。那里健康的美国人可能不得不等待。 
注意：在分阶段疫苗推出中考虑的组之间的共享。资料来源：Surgo基金会和Ariadne实验室
  即使有足够的疫苗可以覆盖所有美国人，细节也不明确。根据美国国家科学，工程和医学研究院的建议，在家工作的健康成年人的风险较低，因此在通勤后他们可以接种疫苗。该组织警告说，如果数量限制超过几个月，则可能需要抽奖。
 而且，当然，这一切都取决于在需要的地方购买疫苗。
 很难理解部署的复杂程度。 C.D.C.说，对于每100剂，提供者将需要105个不同大小的针头和注射器，210个酒精准备垫，4个手术口罩，2个面罩和100个疫苗报告卡，以追踪患者的疫苗历史。设备的生产，包装和运输必须与疫苗交付时一致。
 默克公司疫苗生产前负责人维杰·萨曼特（Vijay Samant）说：“整个运送过程非常繁琐。” “我认为您不会在头六个月内产生有意义的疫苗接种影响。”
 后勤挑战还不止这些。例如，华盛顿州表示，仅在头两个月的疫苗接种中，就需要多达20,000磅的干冰。
 而且由于最有前途的疫苗需要两剂，因此当人们将在21至28天后再次接种第二剂疫苗时，所需的设备要多一倍。 
萨曼特说：“这是一种完全不切实际的疫苗。” “但是我们现在处在一个不切实际的情况下，所以不管可行，可行。”
 对于在疫苗行业工作了25年以上的萨曼特先生来说，挑战似乎是巨大的。他将其与水痘疫苗进行了比较，水痘疫苗在1995年发布时也需要一条新的冷藏链。默克购买了用于儿科的冷冻柜，这是一项复杂的推广计划的一部分。
 他说：“那是一项了不起的努力，而且计划在发射之前至少进行18个月的计划。”
  大多数人已经发布了疫苗接种计划，概述了他们将如何获得和分发疫苗。根据Kaiser Family Foundation的审查，一些企业已经开始与可以分发疫苗的供应商建立合作关系，而其他企业仍在制定这些计划。
 默克公司疫苗部门前总裁戈登·道格拉斯说：“有些州的情况要比其他州好得多。” “很遗憾，这是事实。”
 与加利福尼亚州和马里兰州等州相比，爱达荷州，西弗吉尼亚州和阿拉巴马州的计划包含的细节很少。例如，科罗拉多州的计划长达100多页；蒙大拿州是35岁。
 两种领先疫苗的储存要求使后勤方面的挑战变得更加严峻，这两种疫苗都必须在超冷温度下储存-一种在负80摄氏度下保存。 （由阿斯利康和牛津大学共同研制的第三种疫苗，其早期结果令人质疑，它使用了更熟悉的技术，因此更易于推广。） 
宾利大学自然生物学和应用科学教授弗雷德·莱德利（Fred Ledley）说：“分发的东西必须在80℃冷藏，这是可以做到的，人们知道如何制造冰柜。” “但是它非常昂贵。”而且您当地的药店还无法做到。
 在他们工作的医院中，为卫生保健工作者和急救人员接种疫苗相对容易。但是需要花费时间才能接触到其他重要工人，甚至需要更长的时间才能到达普通人群。
 在您当地的社区中心或学校体育馆，拐弯处会有长长的蛇行。想一想典型选举中的情况。
 当您考虑阴谋理论家和游击党议员如何在这次选举的投票地点抓住次要问题作为普遍欺诈的证据时，这是一个不祥的比较。就像选举日一样，全国性的疫苗接种工作将涉及成千上万的人在显微镜下工作。每个小小的错误都可能被证明是不值得信任的疫苗。
 萨曼特先生说：“您谈论的是人们在计算票数时出错，这很容易，黑白相间。” “在这里，您要人们提供两剂疫苗，这是一个非常复杂的过程。”
 注意：“养老院中的老年人”包括长期护理机构中的老年人。基本工人包括由国土安全部分类的工人。 Stuart A. Thompson（@stuartathompson）是Opinion的作家和编辑。
note_en:
A vaccine may be around the corner, but how long will it be until you get the shot? Health officials are considering vaccine timelines that give some Americans priority over others. If you’re a healthy American, you may wait many months for your turn.
 To put this in perspective, we worked with the  Surgo Foundation and  Ariadne Labs using their  vaccine tool to calculate the number of people who will need a vaccine in each state and county — and where you might fit in that line.
   How old are you?    What county do you live in?    Do you work in these professions?    Do you have Covid-related health risks?
 Based on your risk profile, we believe you’re in line behind   people across the United States.
 When it comes to  , we think you’re behind   others who are at higher risk in your state.
  If the line in   was represented by about 100 people, this is where you’d be standing:
    The order outlined above is one possibility, combining proposals by the  Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention alongside a fuller proposal by the  National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine.
 The final order is not yet determined and depends on successful vaccines being adequately tested for every group. Children might be skipped if early vaccines aren’t approved for  people under 18. States could also set their priorities, but will most likely follow the final C.D.C. recommendations.
 How quickly we’ll move through the final line is also an open question.
 While millions of health care workers in the country could be vaccinated this month, the most prominent vaccine candidates require an ultracold distribution chain that can’t yet reach every American. States also need to procure even more personal protective equipment and set up socially distanced mass-vaccination sites amid a pandemic that could slow everything down.
 “It’s incredible that we have vaccines with high levels of initial efficacy in such a short period of time,” said Dr. Sema Sgaier, a co-founder and the executive director of the Surgo Foundation.
 “But the vaccine is not going to be a silver bullet for a while,” she added.
  The rollouts are based on who’s most at risk and how we can most effectively lower transmission and reduce deaths. Adults aged 18 to 30 should come earlier, NASEM recommended, because they may be responsible for more asymptomatic transmission.
 It’s possible enough vaccine could be available after the first few months to skip phases altogether and start vaccinating everyone. But any delay in approvals, manufacturing or distribution could mean giving some Americans priority over others for much of next year.
 “I don’t think there’s anyone in public health who thinks we have the human capacity and the logistical capacity to handle everyone in just a few months,” said Dr. William Schaffner, a professor of infectious diseases at the Vanderbilt University School of Medicine.
 And in a country as divided as America, it seems inevitable that the process will get politicized. The idea that some of us should take precedence over others is likely to run up against America’s me-first libertarian streak, the same one that flouted mask-wearing laws and rejected scientific recommendations in favor of conspiracy theories and rumor.
 Several groups, including the American Medical Association, have argued that the 2.3 million incarcerated people should be vaccinated before other healthy Americans. That’s for good reason: People in prison have a 5.5-fold greater risk of developing Covid-19, according to  a study of state and federal prisons. It’s also often impossible to practice social distancing in prison.
 An outbreak in a prison or jail also spreads easily to the community.  One study found that by mid-April, roughly one in six coronavirus cases in Illinois were linked to people released from Cook County Jail.
 The idea that prisoners could get vaccinated before average Americans is already facing pushback. “There’s no way it’s going to go to prisoners before it goes to people who haven’t committed any crime,” Gov. Jared Polis of Colorado said this week.
 “Whichever criteria people choose, there will always be someone not happy with it,” Dr. Sgaier said. “We should definitely expect pushback and people unhappy with the process. That’s going to be political.”
 The prioritization will seem especially pronounced in states with a larger share of incarcerated and high-risk Americans, including many Southern states, like Alabama, Louisiana, Texas and Mississippi. Healthy Americans there may have to wait.
 Note: Share among groups considered in phased vaccine rollout. Source: Surgo Foundation and Ariadne Labs
  Even when there is enough vaccine to reach all Americans, the particulars are murky. Healthy adults working from home have lower risk, so they may get vaccinated after people who commute, according to a recommendation by the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine. If quantities are limited for longer than a few months, a lottery might be necessary, the group warned.
 And, of course, all this depends on getting a vaccine where it needs to go.
 It’s hard to grasp how complicated the rollout will be. For every 100 doses, providers will need 105 needles and syringes of varying sizes, 210 alcohol prep pads, 4 surgical masks, 2 face shields and 100 vaccine report cards to track patients’ vaccine histories, according to the C.D.C. That equipment must be manufactured, packaged and shipped to coincide with vaccine deliveries.
 “The whole delivery process is humongous,” said Vijay Samant, a former head of vaccine manufacturing for Merck. “I don’t think you’ll make a meaningful vaccination impact in the first six months.”
 The logistical challenges don’t end there. Washington State, for instance, indicated it would need up to 20,000 pounds of dry ice — for the first two months of vaccinations alone.
 And since the most promising vaccines require two doses, twice as much equipment is needed for a full vaccination, when people will return for a second dose 21 to 28 days later.
 “This is a completely impractical vaccine,” Mr. Samant said. “But we're in an impractical scenario right now, so whatever works, works.”
 For Mr. Samant, who worked in the vaccine business for more than 25 years, the challenge seemed mammoth. He compared it with the chickenpox vaccine, which also required a new cold storage chain when it was released in 1995. Merck purchased freezers for pediatric practices as part of a complex rollout plan.
 “What a remarkable effort that was, and it was planned for at least 18 months before the launch took place,” he said.
  Most have released  vaccination plans outlining how they’ll acquire and distribute the vaccines. Some have started partnering with providers that can dole out vaccines, while others are still developing those plans, according to a Kaiser Family Foundation  review.
 “Some states are going to do a hell of a lot better than other states,” said Gordon Douglas, former president of Merck’s vaccine division. “That’s unfortunately the truth.”
 Plans from Idaho, West Virginia and Alabama contain few details compared with those of states like California and Maryland. Colorado’s plan, for instance, runs over 100 pages long; Montana’s is 35.
 The logistical challenges are made worse by the storage requirements for the two leading vaccines, both of which must be stored at ultracold temperatures – one at minus 80 degrees Celsius. (A third vaccine, by AstraZeneca and the University of Oxford, which had more  questionable early results, uses more familiar technology that would be easier to roll out.)
 “Distributing something that has to be refrigerated at minus 80 — it can be done, people know how to make freezers,” said Fred Ledley, a professor of natural biology and applied sciences at Bentley University. “But it’s very expensive.” And your local pharmacy can’t do it yet.
 It will be comparatively easy to vaccinate health care workers and first responders in hospitals where they work. But it will take time to reach other  essential workers and even longer for the general population.
 Expect long lines snaking around the corner at your local community center or school gymnasium. Think of what it looks like during a typical election.
 That’s an ominous comparison when you consider how conspiracy theorists and partisans seized on minor issues at polling sites this election as proof of widespread fraud. And like Election Day, a national vaccination rollout will involve thousands of people working under a microscope. Every little mistake could be twisted into proof that the vaccines shouldn’t be trusted.
 “You’re talking about people making errors in counting votes, which is so easy, black and white,” Mr. Samant said. “And here you’re asking people to deliver a two-dose vaccine, which is a far more complex process.”
 Note: “Elderly in nursing homes” include those in long-term care facilities. Essential workers include those  categorized by the Department of Homeland Security.  Stuart A. Thompson ( @stuartathompson) is a writer and editor for Opinion.
title: 在疫苗专线中找到您的位置
title_en: Find Your Place in the Vaccine Line

本周，谷歌两次因其工作场所问题而成为头条新闻，而《纽约时报》报道了该加密货币初创公司关于种族主义和歧视问题的报道后，Coinbase再次成为新闻焦点。但是，从更积极的方面来看，纳斯达克向SEC提出了对上市公司的多样性要求。
 要在每个星期五下午1点将其直接发送到您的收件箱中PT，请务必在这里注册。
   本周以关于加密货币公司Coinbase的多样性问题的新闻开始。具体来说，《纽约时报》报道了无数内部投诉，指称对黑人雇员的歧视性待遇。这是故事的主要片段：
 “这15名员工在美国最有价值的加密货币初创公司Coinbase工作，他们代表着600名员工中约四分之三的黑人员工。五名了解情况的人士说，在2018年末和2019年初离职之前，至少有11人告知人力资源部或其经理他们所说的种族主义或歧视性待遇。
 在文章发表之前，Coinbase为员工预告了即将发生的故事，称“其中只有三人在Coinbase工作期间提出了投诉”，并且没有证据表明有任何不当行为。
   一群股东对包括People CEO Ben Silbermann在内的Pinterest高管提起诉讼，称他们使歧视文化成为现实。投诉还声称，歧视文化损害了Pinterest的声誉，并造成了财务损失。
  Pinterest发言人在接受评论时表示：“ Pinterest的领导层和董事会认真履行其信托职责，并致力于继续努力，以确保Pinterest成为我们所有员工感到包容和支持的地方。” “我们相信，我们已经采取的行动以及对我们的文化，政策和做法的持续独立审查将有助于我们实现为所有人建立多样化，公平和包容的环境的目标。” 
国家劳工关系委员会（National Labour Relations Board）在调查了去年11月解雇了几名员工后，于本周对Google提出了投诉。投诉称Google监视员工违反了《美国国家劳动关系法》的某些规定，并在《美国国家劳动关系法》第7节所保障的权利行使方面进行了一般的干预，约束和强迫。
 投诉指出，NLRB还指控Google劝阻“其员工组建，加入，协助工会或从事其他受保护的一致行动”。
 谷歌发言人在给TechCrunch的一份声明中说：“谷歌一直致力于支持内部讨论的文化，我们对员工非常信任。” “当然，员工保护了我们大力支持的劳工权利，但是我们一直非常重视信息安全。我们对自己的决定和法律地位充满信心。有争议的员工采取的行动严重违反了我们的政策，并且违反了可信赖的责任，这是不可接受的。”
 Google道德AI团队的联合负责人表示，她因发送电子邮件而被解雇
  Timnit Gebru是道德和人工智能领域的领先研究人员和声音，他说Google炒掉了她发送给她直接报告的电子邮件。据格布鲁称，谷歌解雇了她，是因为她发给下属的一封电子邮件，该公司称这反映出“行为与谷歌经理的期望相悖。”
 她写道，凯西·牛顿（Casey Newton）获得的有关电子邮件讨论了格布鲁（Gebru）对于她所在的组织如何感到失望的想法，“毕竟，这次谈话”今年只雇用了14％左右的女性。她指出，领导Google Brain团队的一组研究人员的Samy Bengio如何聘用39％的女性，但没有动力激励他这么做。她补充说：
 “我想说的是停止写文档，因为这没有什么区别。我们不知道它们来自何处的DEI OKR（无论如何也从未遇到过），随机讨论，“我们需要更多的指导”，而不是“我们需要阻止有毒的环境阻碍我们前进”，战斗和教育由您自担，没关系。因为问责制为零。雇用39％的女性没有任何动力：当您开始倡导代表性不足的人时，您的生活会变得更糟；当其他领导者不想在校准过程中给您良好的评价时，您就会开始感到沮丧。没有更多的文档或更多的对话将无法实现任何目标。我们刚刚进行了一次黑人研究，如此激动地表现出了愤怒。你知道那之后发生了什么吗？以最基本的方式静音。” 
格布鲁（Gebru）的电子邮件还讨论了让边缘化的声音保持沉默，她的专业知识如何被解雇以及她如何被Google吸引的问题。
  作为提案22的一部分，演出工人承诺为其工人提供医疗补助。本周，Instacart提供了更多有关实践意义的细节。 TL; DR是每季度平均提供给每周平均工作15个小时或以上的购物者。
 每周平均在15到25个小时之间的人有资格获得平均贡献的50％。那些平均时间超过25小时的人将有资格获得平均贡献的100％。
 但需要注意的是，那些由雇主赞助的医疗，Medicare和Medicaid的人没有资格获得补贴。
  纳斯达克向美国证券交易委员会提交了一项提案，以采纳围绕多样性的新规则。如果获得批准，SEC将要求在纳斯达克上市的公司公开报告其董事会的多样性。该规则还要求许多公司至少拥有两名不同的董事：一名自我认定为女性，另一名自我认定为代表性不足的少数群体或LGBTQ +。如果公司不满足该要求，则需要解释原因。
note_en:
This week, Google made headlines a couple of times for its workplace issues, while Coinbase found itself back in the news after the New York Times reported on alleged issues of racism and discrimination at the cryptocurrency startup. On the more positive side, however, Nasdaq proposed to the SEC some diversity requirements for public companies.
 To get this sent directly to your inbox every Friday at 1 p.m. PT, be sure to  sign up here.
   The week kicked off with news about cryptocurrency company Coinbase’s diversity issues. Specifically, the   NYT reported  a myriad of internal complaints alleging discriminatory treatment against Black employees. Here’s a key snippet from the story:
 “The 15 people worked at Coinbase, the most valuable U.S. cryptocurrency start-up, where they represented roughly three-quarters of the Black employees at the 600-person company. Before leaving in late 2018 and early 2019, at least 11 of them informed the human resources department or their managers about what they said was racist or discriminatory treatment, five people with knowledge of the situation said.”
 Before the article was published, Coinbase flagged the upcoming story for its employees, saying “only three of these people filed complaints during their time at Coinbase,” and that there was no evidence of any wrongdoing.
   A group of shareholders filed a lawsuit against Pinterest executives, including CEO Ben Silbermann, alleging they enabled a culture of discrimination. The complaint also alleges that culture of discrimination has harmed Pinterest’s reputation and led to financial harm.
  “Pinterest’s leadership and Board take their fiduciary duties seriously and are committed to continuing our efforts to help ensure that Pinterest is a place where all of our employees feel included and supported,” said a Pinterest spokesperson when reached for comment. “We believe the actions we’ve initiated as well as the ongoing independent review regarding our culture, policies, and practices will help us achieve our goal of building a diverse, equitable and inclusive environment for everyone.”
  The National Labor Relations Board this week  issued a complaint against   Google  after investigating the firing of several employees last November. The complaint alleges Google violated parts of the National Labor Relations Act by surveilling employees, and generally interfered with, restrained and coerced employees in the exercise of their rights guaranteed by   Section 7 of the National Labor Relations Act .
 The NLRB also alleges Google discouraged “its employees from forming, joining, assisting a union or engaging in other protected, concerted activities,” the complaint states.
 “Google has always worked to support a culture of internal discussion, and we place immense trust in our employees,” a Google spokesperson said in a statement to TechCrunch. “Of course employees have protected labor rights that we strongly support, but we have always taken information security very seriously. We’re confident in our decision and legal position. Actions undertaken by the employees at issue were a serious violation of our policies and an unacceptable breach of a trusted responsibility.”
 Google’s co-lead of Ethical AI team says she was fired for sending an email
  Timnit Gebru, a leading researcher and voice in the field of ethics and artificial intelligence, says Google fired her for an email she sent to her direct reports. According to Gebru, Google fired her because of an email she sent to subordinates that the company said reflected “behavior that is inconsistent with the expectations of a Google manager.”
 The email in question,   obtained by Casey Newton , discussed how Gebru was disappointed in how her organization had, “After all this talk,” only hired 14% or so women this year, she wrote. She pointed to how Samy Bengio, who leads a group of researchers inside the Google Brain team, hired 39% women but that there is no incentive for him to do so. She added:
 “What I want to say is stop writing your documents because it doesn’t make a difference. The DEI OKRs that we don’t know where they come from (and are never met anyways), the random discussions, the “we need more mentorship” rather than “we need to stop the toxic environments that hinder us from progressing” the constant fighting and education at your cost, they don’t matter. Because there is zero accountability. There is no incentive to hire 39% women: your life gets worse when you start advocating for underrepresented people, you start making the other leaders upset when they don’t want to give you good ratings during calibration. There is no way more documents or more conversations will achieve anything. We just had a Black research all hands with such an emotional show of exasperation. Do you know what happened since? Silencing in the most fundamental way possible.”
 Gebru’s email also discussed issues with silencing marginalized voices, how her expertise has been dismissed and how she’s felt gaslighted by Google.
  As part of Prop 22, gig workers promised healthcare subsidies for their workers.   This week , Instacart provided some more detail on what that means in practice. TL;DR is that it will be available on a quarterly basis to shoppers who work an average of 15 hours or more per week.
 Those who average between 15 to 25 hours per week will be eligible for 50% of the average contribution. Those that average 25 hours or more will be eligible for 100% of the average contribution.
 But it’s important to note that those with employer-sponsored healthcare, Medicare and Medicaid are not eligible for subsidies.
  Nasdaq filed a proposal  with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission to adopt new rules around diversity. If approved, the SEC would require companies listed on the Nasdaq to publicly report the diversity of its board of directors. The rule would also require many companies to have at least two diverse directors — one who self-identifies as female and one who self-identifies as either a person from an underrepresented minority group or as LGBTQ+. If a company does not meet that requirement, they would need to explain why.
title: 人力资本：Google的劳动力绊倒了

note:
这周发生了很多事情。如果您只是在追赶，那么Equity团队本周度过了愉快的时光，通过一系列近期的早期风险投资进行了解析。如果那是您的果酱，请前往这里。今天的新闻通讯The Exchange正在挖掘后期新闻，尽管规模较小的初创企业仍会出现。
  我们从关于本周初我刚接触的公司的笔记开始：Nextiva。如今，该公司的收入已超过2亿美元，是一家安静的巨人，尤其是它没有沿规模扩张途径获得风险投资。
 鉴于科技媒体中有关融资新闻的讨论频繁，因此与Nextiva谈论如何在不依靠高增长和外部资本的情况下进行规模扩张是一个令人耳目一新的休息。
 在与首席执行官兼联合创始人托马斯·戈尔尼（Tomas Gorny）聊天时，我不得不对公司的历史进行深入挖掘。事情有点像这样：1996年20岁移居加利福尼亚后，戈尔尼（Gorny）在互联网泡沫时期为科技公司工作之后，最终于2001年成立了网络托管公司。这家网络托管公司在2007年将公司卖给了另一家名为Endurance International的公司，该公司在2011年以约10亿美元的价格合并为一家实体，后来公开上市，上个月以30亿美元的价格被私有化。您可以阅读TechCrunch的那篇文章，提到了2010年的Endurance，获得了一些历史记录。
 高尔尼（Gorny）于2008年创立了Nextiva，专注于今天所说的“ UcaaS”，即统一通信即服务。这家初创公司的年经常性收入（ARR）增长至约4000万美元，这时它遇到了一个第三方系统的问题，该系统集成了硬件，支持和服务软件，这引发了其思想的转变。该公司着手建立一个平台。
 Nextiva进行了横向扩展，在其扩展套件中增加了CRM软件，分析和其他功能。并且它有效地成长；从创建团队的资金开始，戈尔尼告诉TechCrunch，即使他用了别人的钱，他也将以同样的方式建立公司。
 平台切换是昂贵的，Nextiva计算出在该项目上花费了1亿美元，并告诉TechCrunch，如果仅专注于原始产品，它可能在短期内能够更快地增长。 
Nextiva花费了很多时间和金钱的平台工作现在已经投入市场，并且在从2016年的1亿美元ARR扩大到今年的2亿美元之后，该公司现在认为自己已经完成了向平台地位的演进。实际上，每家公司都希望成为一个平台，这使我有些头疼。而且几乎没有。
 但是，戈尔尼对他的思考使我有些摇摆。他解释说，Nextiva构建了一套产品，但当时还不是一个平台。正确。但是，他认为，该公司在构建一个系统时为其创建了一个系统，该系统为其所有应用程序和服务创建了一个共享的客户数据池，从而使Nextiva能够在其基础层之上更快地进行构建。根据技术滥用之前的平台定义，这似乎很公平。
 Nextiva的下一步是什么？它每年以30％以上的速度增长，可以公开发行。鉴于它是自筹资金的，因此从定义上讲它不会有恐怖的现金消耗，也不能满足IPO的必要基准。更重要的是，尽管戈尔尼（Gorny）确实强调说私有化可以让他的公司在想更多地专注于产品工作时加速和减速增长，但我印象中Nextiva希望变得更加知名。 IPO将对此有所帮助。
 据说2021年将成为独角兽首次公开募股热潮的到来。也许其中一些首次亮相也是黑马。
  本周，我们有三个主题可供我们讨论更广阔的创业市场，值得讨论：AI筹款，金融科技和私人市场流动性。
 在AI方面，最近一直很忙，尤其是在后期阶段。总部位于俄亥俄州的医疗保健AI公司Olive筹集了2.255亿美元，约占其迄今筹集的4.56亿美元的一半。 Olive是一家独角兽，PitchBook将其新估值定为15亿美元。
 很高兴看到中西部地区取得胜利。但是奥利夫并不孤单。 Scale AI也筹集了大笔资金，这次是1.55亿美元，估值为35亿美元。去年，它以超过10亿美元的估值筹集了1亿美元。在AI创业领域的其他地方，Versatile筹集了2000万美元，而Ultimate.ai筹集了2000万美元。忙！ 
踩着脚踏板。 Stripe放弃了许多银行即服务的工具，将这家估值很高的支付公司从最初的利基转移到了更广阔的领域，并可能带来了丰厚的利润。
 那么，对于在相同问题空间中工作的小型创业公司来说，注定会失败吗？如果他们有话要说的话。我曾写过一篇关于通过API提供银行服务的创业公司Treasury Prime首席执行官克里斯·迪恩（Chris Dean）致信The Exchange，他说：“（来自Stripe新闻）最重要的信号是向银行发出” “需要开放的银行API来保持相关性。”
 而且Dean估计，由于每家金融科技公司都有多个供应商来处理不同的事物，因此通过API银行服务为每家主要金融科技公司提供大量供应商的空间，并指出Treasury Prime拥有“使用Marqeta，Galileo和Stripe的客户满足他们的银行需求。
 让我们来看看;但Stripe新闻始终是大新闻。我认为，新的更新说明了IPO等待。当这些新事物推动增长时，最好公开上市。上周我们的笔记太多了，因为它们对IPO滞后进行了审查。
 最后，CartaX。我对这则新闻感到非常兴奋。 Carta可帮助初创企业管理其资产负债表，并由员工处理其股权，目前正在建立各种交易所，这些交易所应为私人市场带来更多的流动性，从而带来更多的定价信号，并为透明性祈祷。明年年初就要来了。这里更多。
  我们的房间很矮，所以本周只剩下三件事：
 Scale Venture Partners筹集了价值6亿美元的新基金。比上一个基金增加了50％。 
收盘时，联交所赶上了Yext首席执行官霍华德·勒曼（Howard Lerman）的最新收益报告，该报告超出了对其第三季度业绩的近期预期，但让投资者在第四季度的指导上想要更多。
 与Lerman聊天-前几天加入TechCrunch参加Extra Crunch Live-我获得了成功。一方面，Yext致力于提供搜索服务的努力正在发挥作用，推动了新的徽标领域的发展，并帮助其削减了销售过程中的成本。另一方面，世界正在重新进入停工状态，这意味着该公司在某些地区正面临销售疲软的局面，削弱了其短期净保留率的结果，这是软件公司增长的主要动力。
 现在，Yext是一家单一的SaaS上市公司，所以我不想对其结果进行过度索引，但是该公司对其在近期增长方面面临的不确定性的诚实评估并不是其运营所独有的。 –在我们向初创公司询问几周后的第四季度增长时需要考虑的问题。
 就其价值而言，Yext出现在智能产品扩展之中，而其部分市场却因宿醉而陷入困境。初创公司说，这种情况最适合私人时度过。也许Yext将成为在公开场合如何应对相同情况的可行案例。
note_en:
So very much happened this week. If you are just catching up, the Equity crew spent good time this week parsing through a host of recent early-stage venture capital rounds; if that’s your jam, head  here. Today for the newsletter The Exchange is digging into later-stage news, though smaller startups will still make an appearance.
  We begin with notes on a company that was net-new to me when I met it earlier this week:  Nextiva. Now north of $200 million in revenue, the company is a quiet giant and, notably, has not taken venture capital funding along its path to scale.
 Given how frequently conversation in the tech press concerns funding news it was a refreshing break to talk to Nextiva about how it managed to scale without leaning on high-burn growth and external capital.
 Chatting with CEO and co-founder Tomas Gorny, I got to dig a little under the skin of the company’s history. It goes a little something like this: After moving to California in 1996 at the age of 20, Gorny eventually founded a web hosting company in 2001 after working for tech companies during the dot-com boom. The web hosting company wound up selling to another company  called Endurance International in 2007, which sold as a combined entity for around a billion dollars in 2011, later going public before  being taken private last month for $3 billion — you can read this  TechCrunch piece that mentions Endurance from 2010 for a bit of the historical record.
 Gorny founded Nextiva in 2008, focused on what it describes today as “UcaaS,” or unified communications as a service. The startup grew to about $40 million in annual recurring revenue (ARR), at which point it ran into issues with a third-party system that would integrate hardware, and support and services software, which sparked a shift in its thinking. The company set out to build a platform.
 Nextiva expanded horizontally, adding CRM software, analytics and other functionality to its broader suite as it scaled. And it grew efficiently; starting with money from its founding team, Gorny told TechCrunch that even if he had used someone else’s money, he would have built the company in the same manner.
 The platform switch was expensive, with Nextiva calculating that it spent $100 million on the project, telling TechCrunch that it might have been able to grow more quickly in the short-term if it had only focused on its original offerings.
 The platform work that Nextiva has spent so much time and money on is  now in the market, and after scaling  from $100 million ARR in 2016 to $200 million this year, the company now considers itself to have completed the evolution to platform status. Which raised my hackles slightly, as quite literally every company wants to be a platform. And nearly none of them are.
 Gorny, however, swayed me somewhat with his thinking on the matter. Nextiva built a suite of products, he explained, but wasn’t a platform at that point. Correct. However, he argued that the company became one when it built a system that created a shared pool of customer data for all its apps and services, allowing Nextiva to build faster on top of its foundational layer. By the definition of platform that precedes tech’s abuse of the word, that seems fair.
 What’s next for Nextiva? Growing at more than 30% a year, it could go public. Given that it is self-funded, it cannot have horrorshow cash burn by definition and meet requisite benchmarks for an IPO. Even more, while Gorny did highlight that being private allowed his company to accelerate and decelerate growth when it wanted to focus more on product work, I got the impression that Nextiva wants to be better-known. And an IPO would help with that.
 2021 is said to be a coming ground for a stampede of unicorn IPOs. Perhaps some of those debuts will be dark horses as well.
  We have three themes this week for our discussion of the broader startup market that warrant discussion: AI fundraising, fintech and private-market liquidity.
 On the AI front, it’s been a busy sector of late, especially amongst the later-stages. Ohio-based healthcare AI company  Olive raised $225.5 million, or about half of the $456 million that it has raised to date. Olive is a unicorn, to boot, with  PitchBook pegging its new valuation at $1.50 billion on a post-money basis.
 It’s nice to see a win for the Midwest. But Olive was hardly alone. Scale AI also raised a huge chunk of money, this time  $155 million at a $3.5 billion valuation. Last year it  raised $100 million at a valuation north of $1 billion. And elsewhere in the AI startup realm,  Versatile raised $20 million, and  ultimate.ai raised $20 million. Busy!
 Scooting along. Stripe  dropped a host of banking-as-a-service tooling, shifting the richly valued payments company from its initial niche into a much broader — and potentially lucrative — domain.
 So, doom for smaller startups working in the same problem space? Not if they have anything to say about. Chris Dean, the CEO of  Treasury Prime, a startup that I’ve  written about that offers banking services via an API, wrote in to The Exchange, saying that the “most significant signal [from the Stripe news] is to banks” that they “need an open banking API to stay relevant.”
 And Dean reckons that as every fintech has multiple vendors for different things, there will be room for many vendors per major fintech served by banking-via-an-API services, noting that Treasury Prime has “clients who use Marqeta, Galileo, and Stripe” for their banking needs.
 Let’s see; but the Stripe news  is big news all the same. And the new updates explain the IPO wait, I reckon. Better to go public when it has these new pieces driving growth. So much  for our notes last week that were censorious concerning its IPO lag.
 Finally, Carta X. I am bursting with excitement about this bit of news. Carta, which helps startups manage their cap table and employees handle their equity stakes, is building an exchange of sorts that should bring more liquidity — and therefore more pricing signal, and, pray, transparency — to the private markets. It’s coming early next year.  More here.
  We’re low on room, so just three final things to close out this week:
 Scale Venture Partners raised a new fund  worth $600 million. That’s up 50%  from its last fund.
 And, to close,  The Exchange   caught up with  Yext CEO Howard Lerman about its recent earnings report, which  bested near-term expectations regarding its Q3 results, but  left investors wanting more when it came to Q4 guidance.
 Chatting with Lerman — who  joined TechCrunch for an Extra Crunch Live the other week — I got the lay of the land. On one hand, Yext’s push into offering search services is working, driving new logo lands, and helping it trim costs in its sales process. On the other, the world is reentering a shutdown, which means that the company is seeing upsell weakness in certain geographies, denting its near-term net retention results, a key driver of growth for software companies.
 Now,  Yext   is a single public SaaS company, so I don’t want to over-index on its results too much, but the company’s honest assessment of the uncertainties that it faces in terms of near-term growth cannot be unique to its operations — something to consider as we ask startups about their Q4 growth in a few weeks’ time.
 For what it’s worth, Yext appears in the midst of an intelligent product expansion while parts of the market it sells into struggle with a macro hangover. This is the situation that startups say is best to weather while private. Perhaps Yext will become a working case for how to navigate the same circumstance while public.
title: 一家公司如何在没有风险投资的情况下实现2亿美元的收入
title_en: How one company reached revenues of $200M without VC money

在postgres中生成v4 uuid的主要功能有两个，uuid_generate_v4和gen_random_uuid。在postgres 13中，gen_random_uuid是一个内置函数。否则，您将需要安装pgcrypto扩展。 uuid_generate_v4需要uuid-ossp扩展名。取决于postgres的配置方式，postgres可能实际上将不同的库用于uuid-ossp扩展（ossp-uuid，libc，libuuid），有关更多信息，请参见postgres文档。
        为了确定为什么uuid_generate_v4（）在Windows服务器上这么慢，需要进行更多调查。目前尚不清楚enterprisedb postgres将哪个底层库用于uuid-ossp。无论Windows服务器上的速度为何缓慢，目前最好的选择是使用gen_random_uuid，它在所有经过测试的平台上都快得多，并且内置在postgres 13中。
  为确保Windows问题不仅限于edb postgres 12.4版本，我对最新的edb postgres 13进行了一个快速基准测试，发现uuid_generate_v4的执行时间相似。
note_en:
There are two main functions to generate v4 uuids in postgres,  uuid_generate_v4 and  gen_random_uuid. In postgres 13,  gen_random_uuid is a built in function. Otherwise you will need to install the  pgcrypto extension.  uuid_generate_v4 requires the  uuid-ossp extension. Depending how postgres is configured, postgres may actually use different libraries for the  uuid-ossp extension (ossp-uuid, libc, libuuid) see  postgres doc for more info.
        More investigation is required to determine why  uuid_generate_v4() is so slow on windows server. It’s unclear what underlying lib the enterprisedb postgres is using for  uuid-ossp. Regardless of why it’s slow on windows server, the best option for now is to use  gen_random_uuid which is significantly faster on all platforms tested and comes built in on postgres 13.
  To be sure the windows issue was not limited to the edb postgres 12.4 version, I did one quick benchmark on the latest edb postgres 13 and found similar execution times for uuid_generate_v4.

无论您是海龟还是船长，要在地球上寻路都需要两种工具：地图和指南针。
  迭戈加西亚（Diego Garcia）的天空越来越黑，空气变得温暖。当接近满月的最高点时，一只绿海龟将自己的方式划到沙滩上。这只海洋巨人的宽度超过一米，从鼻子到尾巴的长度几乎一样长。她的甲壳上散布着绿色和黑色的斑点，上面沾满了盐水。
  龟在爬行动物中具有一定的优雅风度，但在陆地上笨拙，沉闷的动作唤起了需要更多曲柄的发条玩具。在从水线拖曳一段合适的距离后，乌龟开始挖出一个浅洞，她的手臂和腿像锹一样，将鹅卵石和沙子从空中扔出。几乎精疲力尽后，她终于放松下来，将数十枚乒乓球状的鸡蛋释放到了地面。
  这可能是她多年来第一次在旱地上放脚蹼。除了它们孵化并爬入海浪后的瞬间，海龟在海洋中度过了整个生命。只有雌性返回孵化处所在的海滩上的卵时，它们才离开水面-短暂停留数小时，然后滑回海中。他们可能在交配季节产下几枚卵，然后出发前往觅食地区。他们在那里呆了几年，通过品尝海草来恢复精力，然后回到自己的原始海滩，在近海交配，重新开始新的周期。
  完全履行了母亲的职责后，这只乌龟完成了一项仪式，在印度洋查戈斯群岛的脚印状环礁迭戈加西亚环礁上演了无数次。绿海龟将环礁作为孵化器已有数百或数千代之久，甚至更长。每一代人分散并返回；这些濒临灭绝的爬行动物的去向，走到那里的路线以及它们的航行方式，都是一个谜。
  如此一来，在2017年10月的月夜，来自迭戈加西亚（Diego Garcia）美国军事设施的志愿者帮助斯旺西大学的海洋生物学家兼海龟保护主义者妮可·埃斯特万（Nicole Esteban）将GPS发射器固定在了龟壳的顶部她下了蛋。志愿者给这只乌龟命名为Serenity，看着她和她背上的电脑悄悄回到海浪中消失了。
  三个月后，Serenity在塞舌尔群岛的一个名为Farquhar Atoll的小岛上到达觅食的水域。它在迭戈加西亚（Diego Garcia）以西约四千公里处，但是GPS信号追踪了一条蜿蜒的路线，该路线绕过了六千多公里的远洋。如果她走了一条更直接的路，她可以在不到一个月的时间内完成整个旅程。 
这些现在濒临灭绝的爬行动物的确切位置，走到那里的路线以及它们的航行方式都是一个谜。
  从其他方面来看，这也是一次不寻常的旅程。通常，当生物学家从小岛上的嵌套海滩追踪海龟时，大多数人会飞到沿海地区，划过公海直到撞到大陆架，然后左转或右转。但是Serenity最终落在了一个有斑点的小岛上，她的许多同伙（埃斯特班的团队在五年内给总共35只海龟加了标签）跟进了诉讼。
  “其中许多乌龟都迁移到非常小的岛上目标，有些不超过几百平方米。”迪金大学生物学家亚历克斯·拉特雷（Alex Rattray）说，他也参与了这项研究。少数人确实向西行驶了5000多公里，到达索马里和莫桑比克的海岸线，但另一些人则沿海岸而行，在塞舌尔群岛的其他地方加入了Serenity。还有一些人向北游到马尔代夫群岛。
  他们的目的地突显了海龟迁徙的非凡性质。令人惊讶的是，海龟可以在数千英里的开阔海洋中航行，没有明显的地标，并且可以在正确的地方蜿蜒而行。更加令人惊讶的是，正确的地方是一点点沙土，只有蓝色，直到各个方向的地平线。
  这是使查尔斯·达尔文困惑的壮举。 ＆＃34;即使我们向动物提供了没有任何证据的指南针点的感觉，＆＃34;他写道，例如，我们怎么才能解释仅在一年中的一个季节以前在众神聚集的海龟，这些海龟仅在一年中的一个季节就在升天岛的海岸上找到了通往这片斑点的道路。在大大西洋中部。＆＃34;
  自从达尔文（Darwin）在1873年写下这些话以来，科学家一直试图了解乌龟如何进行这些令人敬畏的旅程。在GPS技术发明之前，穿越全球的水手们依赖于复杂的机械仪器和精确的时计。关于海龟如何完成相同的任务，这是唯一可利用的工具，它们只能存在于自己的大脑和身体中，目前还不得而知。但是生物学家在理解海龟如何找到自己的方式方面已经走了很长一段路。
      在Chagos群岛的嵌套海滩上追踪了35只绿海龟迁徙的动画旅行轨迹。 Hays等/《现代生物学》 
从迭戈加西亚（Diego Garcia）的海滩滑落后的第一个月，宁静（Serenity）记录了将近四千公里。她仍在马达加斯加北部和东部稍远的公海中。 Rattray还不知道，但是当他打开计算机检查她的进度时，他会发现Serenity几乎恰好是她在Farquhar Atoll的最终目的地的经度。只有一个问题：她的纬度是否错误。
  这只乌龟已经错过了她的目标约200公里。这就像从纽约市步行到洛杉矶，而无意间在墨西哥的蒂华纳走了起来。但是她没有向右转并向北游​​泳，而是一直向西走，离目标越来越远。
    无论您是海龟还是船长，要在地球上寻路都需要两种工具：地图和指南针。地图会告诉您相对于其他位置的位置：例如，您开始的位置或您想去的位置。指南针可帮助您保持合理的直线运动。
  有各种设​​置和维护标题的方法，海洋生物学家内森·普特曼（Nathan Putman）说，他研究海龟和鲑鱼的航行。视力好的动物可以像某些鸟类一样，基于太阳光的偏振来定向，也可以像在夜空中的甲虫一样，根据夜空中的星星位置来定向。如果动物可以将其视野与内部时钟相结合，则它们可以将太阳的位置用作指南针，从而说明其在一天中在天空中的移动。人们认为，有时间补偿的太阳罗盘是帝王蝶迁徙时用来维持其航向的工具之一。
  其他动物可能会根据持续吹来的风或海浪传播的方向进行定向。确实，当黑头海龟首次在佛罗里达海滩上孵化时，他们就知道会直接游向迎面而来的海浪，这种策略将它们沉积到了墨西哥湾流中，这是一个较大的洋流网络（称为北大西洋亚热带环流）的一部分。这些洋流从东部沿海一直延伸到南欧和北非，环绕着一个称为Sargasso海的区域。通过留在回旋区内，易受伤害的幼小海龟可以保持相对安全。
  1989年，一次气象fl幸帮助北卡罗莱纳大学教堂山分校的生物学家肯·洛曼证实了小鱼的孵化是以波浪为指导的。那年，雨果飓风暂时使海浪向公海传播，而不是向佛罗里达的大西洋海岸传播。当Lohmann将刚孵出的海龟放到这些条件下时，它们游动起来，就像他们天生的编程指示它们这样做一样-结果，它们走错了路。
  但是，如果乌龟要在一个平静，无风的夜晚孵化而又没有波浪指明方向怎么办？洛曼（Lohmann）将小鱼苗带入实验室以进行查找。在完全黑暗的环境中，没有其他线索可以引导他们，他们向东北游泳，在其自然环境中，它们会安全地将其发射到墨西哥湾流中。然后，当他在水箱周围感应出一个人造磁场时，海龟继续向他们认为是东北方向的方向前进。实际上，由于洛曼（Lohmann）的磁性误导，他们正向相反的方向游泳。结果证实他们确实确实利用了地球磁场。 
海龟在孵化时会采用至少两种策略来找到预先编程在大脑中的方式：波的运动和地球磁场。
    综上所述，罗曼的实验表明，海龟在孵化时至少采用了两种策略来寻找预先编入大脑的方式：波的运动和地球磁场。
  当然，圣地亚哥加西亚附近的洋流与佛罗里达海岸附近的洋流不同。迭戈加西亚（Diego Garcia）出生的海龟被送入南赤道洋流，这是一个更大的系统，称为印度洋涡流。地磁场的参数也不同。但是基本原理保持不变：海龟在出生时就带有一套指令，至少在大多数情况下，它们可以安全地将它们运送到大海中。
  ＆＃34;离岸移徙实际上只是更长的越洋移徙的第一部分，” Lohmann说道，他占据了海龟生命的前五到八年-这个时期有时称为“失落的岁月”，开放的海洋，曾经认为海龟会在海流将它们带到任何地方时懒洋洋地漂流。但是Lohmann意识到这种策略可能是致命的。
  例如，如果佛罗里达州的海龟被动地漂浮在北大西洋亚热带环流上，那么在它们穿越大西洋并到达伊比利亚半岛之后，它们可能会被流向英国的洋流所吸引，在那里，英国的海洋变得极冷海龟。同样，在旋转的环流从非洲海岸瞥了一眼，然后返回美洲，第二股相反方向的水流继续向南流经赤道，并远离其余人口。他们可能会幸免于难，但可能永远不会参与将遗传遗产传给下一代。
  罗曼（Lohmann）怀疑，比单独的磁罗盘更复杂的东西会使海龟处于安全的轨道上。他从佛罗里达海滩上挖出了更多的孵化海龟，并将它们带到实验室进行一系列新的磁位移实验。
  当他将海龟暴露于模仿葡萄牙沿海发现的人工磁场时，它们开始向南游泳，这会降低它们进入寒冷北方水域的机会。当他调整场地以模仿非洲西部公海中的海龟时，海龟向西北方向游动，表面上看也使它们得以留在回旋体内。 
＆＃34;我们使用了以前从未在海洋中出现过的海龟，罗曼强调。他们只是在几个小时前孵化了，我们才把它们暴露在这些遥远的地方。海龟出生时不仅带有磁性指南针，而且还带有磁性地图。
  尽管有这些复杂的系统，但是像Serenity这样的成年海龟确实会犯错误。 ＆＃34;它们具有这些令人赞叹的导航壮举，但它们并不完美，＆＃34;拉特雷说。她从马达加斯加以北的海洋向西行驶了近600公里，然后才意识到自己偏离了路线。
        “追踪研究的局限性在于，它们可以为您提供海龟追踪的轨迹，但它们永远无法准确地告诉您这只海龟在想什么，或者它选择沿着那条特定路线前进的原因，＆＃34;当我请洛曼（Lohmann）复习Serenity和其他乌龟在Esteban的研究中所讲的课程时，他说。
  如果海龟出生时带有其出生时的海洋盆地图，我问他，它们如何偏离航道那么几百公里？甚至只有几只觅食区不在遥远水域中的乌龟，而是在距离迭戈·加西亚（Diego Garcia）仅几十公里的大查戈斯银行（Great Chagos Bank）上，没有以任何类似直路的方式游泳。
  ＆＃34;乌龟有时会选择不走最直接的路线， Lohmann提供。 ＆＃34;沿途可能有觅食区，他们可能希望绕道前往食物丰富的地区。可能还有些地区有捕食者。有时由于海洋学原因，他们可能没有选择最直接的路线。＆＃34;
  Esteban的团队已一一排除了大部分解释。风或海流与海龟何时到达目的地甚至改变方向之间都没有关联。至于停下来吃饭，拉特雷说：“他们96％的迁徙时间都花在200米深的水中，因此肯定没有海草。他们不会偏离路线进食。
  一种更可能的解释是，海龟＆＃39;继承的磁图太简单了，以至于可能会出现数十甚至数百公里的偏差。考虑到地球磁场的缺陷，这种演化使他们获得了一个相当粗糙的地图，这并不令人惊讶。 
该磁场是由液化铁和镍在行星核心内的运动形成的，这种现象称为磁流体动力学，地球物理学家仍在努力充分理解这一现象。由于这些金属有些不规则地旋转，因此产生的磁场也会变得有些不均匀。
  尽管存在局部不均匀性，但地球磁场仍然可以广泛预测。它的强度各不相同，赤道附近的拉力较小，两极附近的拉力较强。它的倾斜度或磁场线与行星表面相交的角度也不同。在赤道附近，倾斜角很浅。在两极附近，它几乎变成垂直的。
  如果海龟仅对这些参数之一（强度或倾斜度）敏感，那么它们可以沿大致的南北轴得出其位置。由于海龟可以检测到这两个特征，因此它们也可以得出其东西轴上的位置。海龟使用的双坐标网格与纬度和经度并不完全相同，但是其工作方式相似。这似乎是预先编程的，即计算机随附的软件。普特曼说。 ＆＃34;这有点荒唐。
         不久，宁静来到了以巨型陆龟闻名的阿尔达布拉环礁。 ＆＃34;一个月，这只乌龟坐在亚达伯拉（Aldabra）西边，基本上只是绕圈转圈，响尾蛇回忆。 ＆＃34;每天早晨，我都会去办公室喝咖啡，然后看看乌龟去了哪里。他认为阿尔达布拉（Aldabra）是她的目标，而她在整理相对于她自己的精确位置时遇到了一些麻烦。
  然后是一天，宁静之路突然改变方向，开始几乎直线直线移动到东约600公里的法古哈环礁。她离开迭戈·加西亚（Diego Garcia）后近三个月，终于在除夕到达了目的地。
  得益于极其精确的卫星技术，您可以轻松准确地看到Serenity在任何特定时刻的位置。但是由于乌龟本人依赖于围绕着不规则且不断变化的磁性参数集构建的心理图，普特曼怀疑，这可能只是花了她一段时间，才使她意识到自己处于错误的位置。
  我无法从中得出任何逻辑，拉特雷说。他无法识别乌龟可能用来推断需要进行航向校正的任何海洋学或大气学线索。 ＆＃34;我们的证据表明，他们找不到精确的目标。他们努力寻找非常孤立的岛屿目标，＆＃34;埃斯特万（Esteban）添加。 ＆＃34;但是，即使它们超调了1000公里，他们还是最终找到了它们。 
有一件事很清楚：从一开始，宁静和其他海龟都想到了一个特定的目的地。 ＆＃34;通常，他们会绕过大型觅食地点，而只是继续前进，＆＃34;埃斯特万说。例如，没有特别的原因，Serenity可以将自己的时间花在Aldabra上觅食，而不是继续前往Farquhar Atoll。 Rattray怀疑乌龟可能会在幼年“失落的岁月”中返回他们确定的区域。作为良好的栖息地，食肉动物很少，食物很多。
  “真正的救赎是他们总是设法弄清楚自己在哪里以及如何到达自己要去的地方，”拉特雷说。 “这巩固了他们作为这些出色的航海家的声誉。”它还为保护海龟提供了宝贵的见识，现在几乎所有物种都面临灭绝的威胁。
  ＆＃34;如果要灭绝大量的黑头,，我们将无法从其他地方拿起黑头turtle卵并将其移动来营救它们，＆＃34;罗曼说。例如，佛罗里达州的海龟会孵化出北大西洋的特定航行指示。如果他们搬到日本的海滩，他们的直觉可能导致他们走向灾难。这意味着＆＃34;这些种群中的每一个都是独特的，从保护的角度来看，必须作为一个单独的实体来对待，＆＃34;他说。与其考虑濒临灭绝的物种，不如考虑濒临灭绝的种群。
  埃斯特万（Esteban）补充说，海龟还可以将研究人员带到以前未知的关键栖息地。她说，塞舌尔现在承诺将其专属经济区的30％用于海洋保护，并且他们正在使用海龟跟踪数据来确定哪些位置最重要的保护地区。保护乌龟也具有保护作用：它们所依赖的海草草甸是幼鱼的重要避难所，因此有助于维持渔业，并且对于碳固存具有重要作用。
  与此同时，很久以前，在她第一次到达Farquhar Atoll后约四个月，装在Serenity外壳背面的GPS装置内的电池就没电了。没有人确切知道她现在的位置，但是很有可能她仍然在该地区，懒洋洋地嚼着海草。在接下来的几年中的某个时候，她将结束往返迭戈·加西亚的旅程，并再次开始整个过程​​。任何人都怀疑她是否进行同样曲折的迁徙，或沿着更直的道路游泳。

note_en:
Whether you're a sea turtle or a ship's captain, finding your way around the planet requires two tools: a map and a compass.
  The air was warm as the skies grew dark over Diego Garcia. As the nearly full moon reached its highest point, a green sea turtle scuttled her way onto the sand. The ocean giant was more than a meter wide and nearly as long from nose to tail. Her carapace, mottled with splotches of green and black, was slick with salt water.
  Turtles glide through the sea with a certain reptilian elegance, but on land their awkward, plodding movements evoke a wind-up toy in need of a few more cranks. After shuffling a suitable distance from the waterline, the turtle began to excavate a shallow hole, using her arms and legs like spades to fling pebbles and sand through the air. Nearly exhausted, she finally began to relax as she released dozens of ping-pong ball-sized eggs into the ground.
  It was likely the first time in years she’d set flipper on dry land. Other than the moments after they hatch and crawl into the surf, sea turtles spend their entire lives in the ocean. Only when females return to lay eggs on the same beaches where they hatched do they leave the water—just briefly, for a few hours, before slipping back into the sea. They may lay several clutches of eggs during the mating season before setting off for their foraging territories. There they stay for several years, regaining energy by feasting on seagrass, before returning to their natal beach, mating just offshore, and beginning the cycle anew.
  Having carried out the full extent of her duties as a mother, this turtle had completed a ritual that’s played out countless times on Diego Garcia, a footprint-shaped atoll in the Indian Ocean's Chagos Archipelago. Green sea turtles have used the atoll as an incubator for hundreds or thousands of generations, or perhaps longer. Each generation disperses and returns; precisely where these now-endangered reptiles go, what route they take to get there, and how they navigate, is a mystery.
  And so on that moonlit night in October of 2017 volunteers from the U.S. military facility on Diego Garcia helped  Nicole Esteban, a marine biologist and sea turtle conservationist at Swansea University, fasten a GPS transmitter to the top of the turtle's shell while she laid her eggs. The volunteers nicknamed the turtle Serenity and watched as she and the computer on her back crept back into the waves and disappeared.
  Three months later, Serenity reached her foraging waters along a small island called Farquhar Atoll in the Seychelles archipelago. It is some four thousand kilometers west of Diego Garcia, but the GPS signals traced a circuitous route that wound through more than six thousand kilometers of open ocean. Had she had taken a more direct path, she could have accomplished the entire journey in under a month.
  Precisely where these now-endangered reptiles go, what route they take to get there, and how they navigate, is a mystery.
  It was an unusual trip in other ways, too. Typically when biologists track turtles from their nesting beaches on small islands, most wind up in coastal territory, having paddled across the open ocean until hitting a continental shelf and then turning left or right. But Serenity ended up on a flyspeck island, and many others of her cohort—Esteban’s team tagged a total of 35 turtles over five years—followed suit.
  "A number of these turtles migrated to very, very small island targets, some not more than a couple of hundred meters square,” says Alex Rattray, a biologist at Deakin University who was also involved in the research. A few did travel more than 5000 kilometers west to the coastlines of Somalia and Mozambique, but others pulled up short of the coast, joining Serenity elsewhere in the Seychelles archipelago; still others swam north to the Maldives islands.
  Their destinations underscored the extraordinary nature of sea turtle migration. It’s astonishing enough that a sea turtle can navigate across thousands of miles of open ocean, with no discernible landmarks, and wind up in the correct place. Even more astonishing is when the correct place is a dot of sand with nothing but blue until the horizon in every direction.
  It's a feat that bewildered Charles Darwin. "Even if we grant to animals a sense of the points of the compass, of which there is no evidence," he wrote, "how can we account, for instance, for the turtles which formerly congregated in multitudes, only at one season of the year, on the shores of the Isle of Ascension, finding their way to that speck of land in the midst of the great Atlantic Ocean."
  Since Darwin wrote those words in 1873, scientists have tried to understand just how turtles make these awesome journeys. Before the invention of GPS technology, sailors crossing the globe relied on a combination of complex mechanical instruments and accurate timepieces. Much remains unknown about how turtles accomplish the same task, the only tools at their disposal resting within their own brains and bodies—but biologists have come a long way in understanding how sea turtles find their way.
      The animated travel tracks of 35 green sea turtle migrations tracked from nesting beaches in the Chagos archipelago. Hays et al./ Current Biology
    In the first month after slipping off the beaches of Diego Garcia, Serenity had logged nearly four thousand kilometers. She was still in the open ocean, north and a little east of Madagascar. Rattray wouldn't know it yet, but when he switched his computer on to check on her progress, he would find Serenity at almost precisely the longitude of her final destination in  Farquhar Atoll. There was just one problem: was she was at the wrong latitude.
  The turtle had missed her target by some two hundred kilometers. That would be like walking from New York City to Los Angeles, but accidentally winding up in Tijuana, Mexico instead. But rather than take a right turn and swim north, she kept heading west, further and further away from her goal.
    Whether you're a sea turtle or a ship's captain, finding your way around the planet requires two tools: a map and a compass. A map tells you where you are relative to some other location: where you started out, for example, or where you want to go. A compass helps to keep you moving in a reasonably straight line.
  "There's all sorts of ways to set and maintain a heading," says marine biologist  Nathan Putman, who studies navigation in sea turtles and salmon. Animals who have good vision can orient based on the polarization of sun light, as some birds are thought to do, or based on the position of stars in the night sky, as in dung beetles. If animals can marry their vision to an internal clock, then they can use the sun's position as a compass, accounting for its movement across the sky throughout the day. It's thought that a time-compensated sun compass, as it is called, is one of the tools that migrating monarch butterflies use to maintain their headings.
  Other animals might orient based on the direction that persistent winds blow or the direction waves travel through the ocean. Indeed, when loggerhead sea turtles first hatch on Florida beaches, they know to swim directly into oncoming waves, a strategy that deposits them into the Gulf Stream, part of a larger network of currents called the North Atlantic Sub-tropical Gyre. These currents, which stretch from the eastern seaboard across to southern Europe and northern Africa, encircle a region known as the Sargasso Sea. By staying within the gyre, vulnerable young sea turtles can remain relatively safe.
  In 1989 a meteorological fluke helped Ken Lohmann, a biologist at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, confirm that hatchlings use waves as a guide. That year, Hurricane Hugo temporarily caused waves to travel towards the open ocean rather than towards Florida's Atlantic coast. When Lohmann dropped newly hatched turtles into these conditions, they swam into the waves, just as their innate programming instructed them to—and as a result, they went the wrong way.
  But what if turtles were to hatch on a calm, windless night with no waves to point the way? Lohmann brought hatchlings into a laboratory to find out. In complete darkness and with no other cues available to guide them, they swam towards the northeast, which in their natural environs would have safely launched them into the Gulf Stream. When he then induced an artificial magnetic field around the tanks, the turtles continued in what they thought was a northeasterly direction. In reality, thanks to Lohmann's magnetic misdirection, they were swimming in precisely the opposite direction. The results confirmed that they really did use Earth's magnetic field.
  Sea turtles hatch with at least two strategies for finding their way pre-programmed into their brains: the movement of waves and the Earth's magnetic field.
    Taken together, Lohmann’s experiments revealed that sea turtles hatch with at least two strategies for finding their way pre-programmed into their brains: the movement of waves and the Earth's magnetic field.
  Currents around Diego Garcia are of course different from those off the Florida coast. Turtles born in Diego Garcia are delivered into the South Equatorial Current, which is part of a larger system called the Indian Ocean Gyre. The parameters of Earth's magnetic field differ as well. But the underlying principle remains the same: turtles are born with a set of instructions that, at least most of the time, safely delivers them into the open ocean.
  "Offshore migration is really just the first part in a longer transoceanic migration,” says Lohmann, one that occupies the first five to eight years of a sea turtle’s life—a period sometimes called “the lost years,” spent in the open ocean, where sea turtles were once thought to lazily drift wherever the currents took them. But Lohmann realized that this strategy could be deadly.
  If turtles in Florida floated passively on the North Atlantic Sub-tropical Gyre, for example, then after they cross the Atlantic and approach the Iberian Peninsula, they could be caught in currents that flow towards the United Kingdom, where the ocean becomes lethally cold for a sea turtle. Likewise, where the gyre glances off the coast of Africa and returns to the Americas, a second current flowing the opposite direction continues south past the equator and away from the rest of the population. They might survive such a journey, but would likely never participate in passing their genetic heritage to the next generation.
  Lohmann suspected that something more sophisticated than a magnetic compass alone keeps sea turtles on a safe trajectory. He scooped more hatchling turtles off Florida beaches and brought them into his laboratory for a new series of magnetic displacement experiments.
  When he exposed the turtles to artificial magnetic fields that mimicked those found off the coast of Portugal, they began swimming south, which would decrease their chances of ending up in frigid northern waters. When he adjusted the field to imitate those in the open ocean west of Africa, the turtles swam towards the northwest, likewise ostensibly enabling them to remain within the gyre.
  "We used turtles that had never been in the ocean before," Lohmann emphasizes. They had only hatched a few hours earlier, "and we just exposed them to these faraway locations." Sea turtles are born not just with a magnetic compass, but also with a magnetic map.
  Despite these sophisticated systems, however, adult turtles like Serenity do make mistakes. "They have these amazing feats of navigation but they're not perfect at it," says Rattray. From the ocean north of Madagascar, she would travel almost 600 kilometers further west before apparently realizing she was off course.
        “One limitation of tracking studies is they can provide you with the track that a turtle followed but they can never tell you exactly what the turtle was thinking or the reasons it chose to go along that particular route," says Lohmann when I ask him to review the courses taken by Serenity and the other turtles in Esteban's study.
  If turtles are born with a map of their natal ocean basin, I ask him, how can they wind up so many hundreds of kilometers off course? Even the few turtles whose foraging territories are located not in faraway waters but on the Great Chagos Bank, just a few dozen kilometers from Diego Garcia, didn't swim in anything resembling a straight path.
  "Turtles may sometimes choose not to go the most direct route," Lohmann offers. "There may be foraging areas along the way, they may wish to detour to areas that are rich in food. There may also be areas that have predators in them. And there sometimes are oceanographic reasons that they may not go on the most direct route."
  One by one, Esteban's team had already ruled most of those explanations out. There was no correlation between wind or current and when turtles arrived at their destination, or even when they changed direction. As for stopping for food, says Rattray, “96 percent of their time in migration is spent in water deeper than two hundred meters, so there's certainly no seagrass. They're not going off course to feed."
  A more likely explanation is that the turtles' inherited magnetic map is simply so crude that deviations of dozens or even hundreds of kilometers are to be expected. That evolution endowed them with a fairly coarse-grained map isn't very surprising, given the imperfections of Earth's magnetic field.
  The field is formed by movements of liquified iron and nickel within the planet’s core, a phenomenon called magneto-hydro-dynamics that geophysicists are still working to fully understand. Because those metals swirl around somewhat erratically, the resulting magnetic field winds up being somewhat uneven as well.
  Despite localized unevenness, though, Earth’s magnetic field is still broadly predictable. It varies in strength, with a weaker pull near the equator and a stronger pull near the poles. It also varies in its inclination, or the angle at which magnetic field lines intersect with the planet’s surface. Near the equator the inclination angle is quite shallow. Near the poles it becomes almost perpendicular.
  If sea turtles were sensitive to just one of these parameters—strength or inclination—then they could derive their position along a roughly north-south axis. Because sea turtles can detect both features, they can also derive their position on an east-west axis. The bi-coordinate grid utilized by sea turtles is not exactly the same as latitude and longitude, but it works in a similar way. "And that seems to be pre-programmed, that's the software that comes with the computer," says Putman. "Which is kind of wild."
         Before long, Serenity had reached Aldabra Atoll, famous for its giant land tortoises. "For a month, this turtle just sat here west of Aldabra and just went around in circles basically," Rattray recalls. "Every morning I would come into my office, have a coffee, and just see where the turtle had gone." He assumed that Aldabra was her target, and that she was having a bit of trouble sorting out its precise location relative to her own.
  Then a day came when Serenity abruptly reversed course and began moving in almost a straight line directly to Farquhar Atoll, some six hundred kilometers to the east. She finally reached her destination on New Year’s Eve, nearly three months after leaving Diego Garcia.
  Thanks to extremely precise satellite technology, it was easy to see exactly where Serenity was at any given moment. But since the turtle herself relied on a mental map built around an irregular and constantly changing set of magnetic parameters, Putman suspects that it might simply have taken her a while before she realized that she was in the wrong spot.
  "I can't make any logic out of it," says Rattray. He can't identify any oceanographic or atmospheric cues that the turtle might have used to infer that a course correction was needed. "Our evidence shows that they don't find their targets with pinpoint accuracy. They struggle to find very isolated island targets," adds Esteban. "But they do eventually find them, even if they overshoot them by a thousand kilometers."
  One thing is clear: from the beginning, Serenity and the other turtles all had a particular destination in mind. "Often they were bypassing great foraging sites, and just continuing on," says Esteban. There's no particular reason, for example, that Serenity couldn't have spent her time foraging at Aldabra rather than continuing on to Farquhar Atoll. Rattray suspects that the turtles are likely returning to areas they identified during their juvenile "lost years" as good habitats with few predators and lots of food.
  "The real redeeming thing about that is they always manage to work out where they are and how to get to where they're going,” says Rattray. “It reinforces their reputation as these amazing marine navigators.” It also offers valuable insight for protecting sea turtles,  nearly every species of which is now threatened with extinction.
  "If a population of loggerheads were to become extinct, we will not be able to rescue them by taking loggerhead turtle eggs from elsewhere and moving them," says Lohmann. Sea turtles in Florida, for example, hatch with specific navigation instructions for the north Atlantic Ocean. Were they relocated to, say, beaches in Japan, their instincts could lead them towards catastrophe. What that means is "each of these populations is unique and, from a conservation perspective, has to be approached as a separate entity," he says. Rather than think of endangered species, we should think of endangered populations.
  Esteban adds that sea turtles can also lead researchers to critical habitats that were previously unknown. The Seychelles are now committed to setting aside thirty percent of their exclusive economic zone for marine conservation, she says, and they're using sea turtle tracking data to identify which locations are most important to protect. Turtle protections also have an umbrella effect: the seagrass meadows they rely on are an important refuge for juvenile fish, thus helping to maintain fisheries, and are valuable for their role in carbon sequestration.
  Meanwhile, the battery inside the GPS unit attached to the back of Serenity’s shell died long ago, around four months after she first arrived at Farquhar Atoll. Nobody knows exactly where she is right now, but chances are good that she's still in the area, lazily munching on seagrass. At some point in the next several years she will conclude her round trip back to Diego Garcia and begin the entire process again. Whether she undertakes an equally meandering migration, or swims along a straighter path, is anybody's guess.
