title: 海龟找到了出路

note:
沃尔特·迪斯尼影业（Walt Disney Studios）于1976年以其如今经典的肢体交换喜剧《怪诞星期五》（Freaky Friday）打动了这个臭名昭著的母狮，乔迪·福斯特（Jodie Foster）和芭芭拉·哈里斯（Barbara Harris）主演的女儿和母亲改变了身体。福斯特和哈里斯的表演均获得了金球奖提名，尽管这部电影的评论参差不齐。该工作室多次回归这种状态，1995年的电视电影重拍，2018年迪斯尼频道的电视音乐剧以及2003年由林赛·罗汉（Lindsey Lohan）和杰米·李·柯蒂斯（Jamie Lee Curtis）担任主角的影片均获得好评。
 人体交换概念的最新动向甚至远非家庭友好。文斯·沃恩（Vince Vaughn）饰演的是一位衰老的连环杀手，在弗拉基（Freaky）与一个不幸的十几岁的女孩交换尸体，这也向经典的青少年杀手电影致敬，例如《十三号星期五》（1980）和《尖叫》（1996）。环球影业于10月在电影节之外首次亮相。这部电影于11月13日星期五在各大影院上映（尽管大流行），获得了好评，到目前为止票房收入达到720万美元，考虑到仍有多少家电影院关闭，这是一个不错的成绩。现在可以在VOD上观看了，可以观看有趣的周末观看节目。
  导演克里斯托弗·兰登（Christopher Landon）对恐怖片并不陌生，他曾执导过超自然活动系列的三部电影。他还是2017年令人兴奋的恐怖/喜剧混搭游戏《欢乐死亡日》（Main Death Day）的策划者，《欢乐死亡日》主要是《尖叫》和《土拨鼠日》的结合，在该片中，姐妹姐妹特蕾莎＆＃34; Tree＆＃34;格尔布曼（乌托邦杰西卡·罗特（Jessica Rothe）的乌托邦）生日那天被一名戴着Babyface面具的杀人犯谋杀，并发现自己一天又一次过世。兰登（Landon）跟进了去年同样有趣的续集《欢乐死亡日2 U》（Happy Death Day 2 U），增加了巧妙的多重宇宙扭曲，并特别向《回到未来II》致敬。因此，怪异的星期五和13日星期五的恐怖/喜剧混搭似乎是自然而然的后续行动。
     17岁的米莉·凯斯勒（Millie Kessler）（凯瑟琳·牛顿（Kathryn Newton），《阻挡者》，Big Little Lies）只是想逃过布利斯菲尔德·高（Blissfield High）嗜血的大厅和民众的残酷对待。但是，当她成为该市臭名昭著的连环杀手（The Butcher）（文斯·沃恩（Vince Vaughn），祖兰德先生和史密斯夫人）的最新目标时，她高年级的生活就变得最少。
 当屠夫神秘的古老匕首使他和米莉在彼此的身体中醒来时，米莉得知她只有24小时的时间才能使自己的身体恢复原状，直到切换永久化，并且她永远被中年疯子所困。唯一的问题是，她现在看起来像个高耸的精神变态者，是全市范围内追捕的目标，而《屠夫》看起来也像她，并将他的屠杀欲望带到了归乡。
 在朋友的帮助下-超醒的Nyla（Celeste O'Connor，《捉鬼敢死队：来世》），超赞的Joshua（Misha Osherovich，Goldfinch）和她迷恋的Booker（Uriah Shelton，进入勇士之门）-Millie参加了比赛时钟逆转了诅咒，而The Butcher发现拥有女性青少年的身材是一点点返校狂潮的完美掩饰。
     在任何给定的电影中，人体交换概念的成功始终取决于其主角，后者必须灵活切换角色。沃恩和牛顿并不令人失望。尽管他身高6＆＃39; 5＆＃34;沃恩（Vaughn）尤其擅长引导自己内心的少女。帧，而不仅仅是在明显的闹剧时刻，例如当他表演Blissfield High吉祥物舞蹈以说服Josh和Nyla确实是他时。他还与母亲Paula（Katie Finneran，Brockmire）和Booker简要介绍了Millie的甜蜜漏洞。他恰当地表达了米莉（Milly）能够撒尿站立的乐趣，并最终变得足够强大，足以承受她的高中折磨者的喜悦。 
另一方面，米莉身上的屠夫表现出强烈的时尚感，并乐于以无辜的方式在每个人的雷达下滑动。高中生，尽管他还必须变得更加强大和虚弱，这使得杀死目标变得更加困难。说到这些目标，Freaky遵循了经典杀手电影的严格道德准则：那些死者，不可避免地做了一些“应得的”事情。就像折磨米莉的卑鄙女孩，或是公开羞辱她的木铺老师一样。
 尽管我非常喜欢这部电影，但即使兰登曾说过这两个世界，我也无法摆脱这种ni琐的感觉，即它的效果不如《欢乐死亡日》电影那么好。相同的DNA。他并没有排除像Jason Voorhees这样的Freaky续集，The Butcher可以永远回来的地方-Millie和Tree终于相遇了。 （记录下来，我是来这里的。）也许是因为您只能做很多事情才能使经典的slasher film tropes看起来很新鲜-特别是因为它们已经获得了惊悚/喜剧类电影获得了巨大的成功：《尖叫》（Scream）系列和乔什·惠顿（Josh Whedon）出色的《丛林客舱》那是一些相当激烈的竞争。
 尽管如此，Landon和他的演员显然仍在爆炸，Landon总是用一点点心投入他的恐怖/喜剧中。例如，树处理母亲的死亡，而米莉失去了父亲，母亲下降了。戒酒以应付。尽管可以预测，但Freaky最终以正确的方式成功地将恐怖，幽默和悲痛混合在一起。
note_en:
Walt Disney Studios struck the proverbial motherlode in 1976 with its now-classic body-swap comedy   Freaky Friday, starring Jodie Foster and Barbara Harris as a daughter and mother who switch bodies. Both Foster and Harris received Golden Globe nominations for their performances, even though the film received mixed reviews.  The studio has returned to that vein multiple times, with a  1995 TV movie remake, a  2018 TV musical for the Disney Channel, and a critically acclaimed  2003 remake with Lindsey Lohan and Jamie Lee Curtis in the lead roles.
 The latest twist on the body-swapping concept is not even remotely family-friendly. Vince Vaughn stars as an aging serial killer who switches bodies with a hapless teenaged girl in   Freaky, which also  pays homage to classic teen slasher movies like  Friday the 13th (1980) and  Scream (1996). Universal Pictures debuted the film at Beyond Fest in October. The film hit theaters (despite the pandemic) on Friday, November 13th, garnering solid reviews and eking out $7.2 million at the box office so far—not a bad showing considering how many movie theaters remain closed. It's now available on VOD, and makes for an entertaining, if familiar-feeling, weekend watch.
  Director Christopher Landon is no stranger to horror, having helmed three films in the  Paranormal Activity franchise. He's also the mastermind behind 2017's delightful horror/comedy mashup,  Happy Death Day—basically a combination of  Scream and  Groundhog Day, in which sorority sister Theresa "Tree" Gelbman (Jessica Rothe,  Utopia) is murdered on her birthday by a killer in a Babyface mask and finds herself reliving that day over and over. Landon followed up with last year's equally entertaining sequel,   Happy Death Day 2 U, which added an ingenious multiverse twist and paid particular homage to  Back to the Future II. So a horror/comedy mashup of  Freaky Friday and  Friday the 13th seems like a natural follow-up.
     Seventeen-year-old Millie Kessler (Kathryn Newton,  Blockers, Big Little Lies) is just trying to survive the bloodthirsty halls of Blissfield High and the cruelty of the popular crowd. But when she becomes the newest target of The Butcher (Vince Vaughn,  Zoolander, Mr. and Mrs Smith), her town’s infamous serial killer, her senior year becomes the least of her worries.
 When The Butcher’s mystical ancient dagger causes him and Millie to wake up in each other’s bodies, Millie learns that she has just 24 hours to get her body back before the switch becomes permanent and she’s trapped in the form of a middle-aged maniac forever. The only problem is she now looks like a towering psychopath who’s the target of a city-wide manhunt while The Butcher looks like her and has brought his appetite for carnage to Homecoming.
 With some help from her friends—ultra-woke Nyla (Celeste O’Connor,  Ghostbusters: Afterlife), ultra-fabulous Joshua (Misha Osherovich,  The Goldfinch) and her crush Booker (Uriah Shelton,  Enter the Warriors Gate)—Millie races against the clock to reverse the curse while The Butcher discovers that having a female teen body is the perfect cover for a little Homecoming killing spree.
     The success of the body-swapping concept in any given film always rests on the shoulders of its leads, who must nimbly switch between characters. Vaughn and Newton do not disappoint. Vaughn especially shines at channeling his inner teenaged girl, despite his hulking 6'5" frame—and not just in the obvious slapstick moments, like when he performs the Blissfield High mascot dance to convince Josh and Nyla it's really him. He also brings out Millie's sweet vulnerability in brief  tête-à- têtes with her mother, Paula (Katie Finneran,  Brockmire) and with Booker. And he aptly conveys Milly's delight at being able to pee standing up, and finally being big and strong enough to stand up to her high school tormenters.
    On the flip side, The Butcher in Millie's body shows a strong fashion sense and relishes being able to slide under everybody's radar as an "innocent" high school student, although he must also come to grips with being considerably stronger and weaker, making it a teensy bit harder to kill his targets. Speaking of those targets,  Freaky hews to the strict morality code of classic slasher movies: those who die, inevitably did something to "deserve" it, like the mean girl who torments Millie, or the wood shop teacher who revels in publicly humiliating her.
 While I very much enjoyed the film, I can't quite shake this niggling feeling that it doesn't work quite as well as the  Happy Death Day movies, even though  Landon has said the two worlds "share the same DNA." He has not ruled out a  Freaky sequel—like  Jason Voorhees, The Butcher could always come back—where Millie and Tree finally meet. (For the record, I am here for that.) Perhaps that's because there's only so much you can do to make the classic slasher film tropes seem fresh—especially since they've already gotten the horror/comedy treatment with the hugely successful  Scream franchise and Josh Whedon's brilliant   Cabin the Woods. That's some pretty stiff competition.
 Still, Landon and his cast are clearly having a blast, and Landon always infuses his horror/comedies with a bit of heart—Tree dealing with the death of her mother, for instance, while Millie has lost her father, and her mother has descended into alcoholism to cope. Predictable though it may be,  Freaky ultimately succeeds in mixing horror, humor, and pathos in just the right measures.

So far in this series, we have looked at the general principle of  gradient descent, and how we computed  backpropagation for each layer in a feedforward neural network, then generalising to look at  backprop in different types of layers in a CNN.
 Now we will take a step back and look at backpropagation in a more general sense -  through a computation graph. Through this we’ll get a general intuition for how the frameworks compute their
 We’ll use the LSTM cell as our motivating example - to continue the task of sentiment analysis on the IMDB review dataset - you can find the code in the accompanying  Jupyter notebook
  Let’s look back at the principles we’ve used in this series:

 Breaking down computations - we can use the  chain rule to aid us in our computation - rather than trying to compute the derivative in one fell swoop, we break up the computation into smaller  intermediate steps.
 Computing the chain rule - when thinking about which intermediate values to include in our chain rule expression, think about the immediate outputs of equations involving       x x    x - which other values get directly affected when we slightly nudge       x x    x?
 One element at a time - rather than worrying about the entire matrix       A A    A, we’ll instead look at an element        A  i j A_{ij}     A         i j ​   . One equation we will refer to time and time again is:
 C  i j =  ∑ k  A  i k  B  k j    ⟺    C = A . B C_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik}B_{kj} \iff C=A.B     C         i j ​     =             k ​      A         i k ​     B         k j ​      ⟺     C  =    A . B
 A useful tip when trying to go from one element to a matrix is to look for summations over repeated indices (here it was k) - this suggests a matrix multiplication.
  C  i j =  A  i j  B  i j    ⟺    C = A ∗ B C_{ij} = A_{ij}B_{ij} \iff C=A*B     C         i j ​     =     A         i j ​     B         i j ​      ⟺     C  =    A  ∗    B
 Sanity check the dimensions - check the dimensions of the matrices all match (the derivative matrix should have same dimensions as the original matrix, and all matrices being multiplied together should have dimensions that align.
 A  computation graph allows us to clearly break down the computations, as well as see the immediate outputs when computing our chain rule.
 We will use the computation graph representation  (shown above) of the LSTM to compute the gradients using backpropagation through time.
